title: AI Agents

headings.title: Agent identity: Role, Goal, and Backstory

body:
Overview

Every Pantheon workspace has at least one AI agent — your team's first worker. Agents are fully custom: you define their role, goal, backstory, and autonomy level. There are no locked-in presets. You build the team you need.

Pantheon supports multiple agents per workspace. Each agent can have its own identity, skills, and Discord channel assignment. Think of it as building a small team of AI specialists for your operation.

Single agent vs. multi-agent

Single agent (the default)

When you complete onboarding, you create one agent with a role and goal you define. It handles every channel in your Discord server plus DMs.

This is the simplest setup and works well for many teams. One agent that does everything.

Multi-agent setup

As your team grows or you want more focused responses, add specialist agents:

- A Support Lead in support-tickets that resolves customer questions quickly
- A Research Analyst in research that investigates questions and synthesizes findings
- An Operations Lead in operations that tracks work and keeps execution moving
- A Comms Manager in comms that handles email follow-ups and scheduling

Each specialist is tuned for its area. They know their boundaries and can delegate to other agents when a question falls outside their expertise.

You can always add more agents later. Start with a single general-purpose agent and see how it handles your day-to-day questions. When you want more focused responses on a specific topic, add a specialist.

Agent identity: Role, Goal, and Backstory

Every agent is defined by three fields inspired by the CrewAI pattern:

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|-------|---------|---------|
| Role | What the agent is — its job title or function | "Customer support specialist" |
| Goal | What the agent aims to accomplish | "Resolve customer questions quickly and accurately" |
| Backstory | Personality, tone, and constraints (optional) | "You are friendly but concise. Always cite sources." |

These fields drive the agent's system prompt and shape how it responds. The more specific you are, the better the agent performs within its domain.

Autonomy levels

Each agent has an autonomy level that controls how independently it acts:

| Level | Label | Behavior |
|-------|-------|----------|
| L1 | Assisted | Asks before taking any action. Presents options and waits for approval. |
| L2 | Copilot | Suggests actions and explains reasoning. Executes read-only operations automatically. |
| L3 | Autopilot | Acts independently based on its goal. Only asks when genuinely ambiguous. |

Skills and schedules

Agents can be given custom skills — Markdown-based instructions that teach them how to handle specific tasks. Create skills from scratch or generate them with AI via the Skill Forge.

Agents can also have custom schedules — cron-based scheduled messages that post to their assigned Discord channel automatically. For example, a daily summary at 8 AM or a weekly report every Monday.

Inter-agent communication

When you have multiple agents, they can delegate tasks to each other using built-in communication tools (agentslist and sessionssend). If someone asks the general assistant a detailed research question, it can hand off to the Research Analyst.

Users see a seamless experience — they ask a question in any channel, and the right agent handles it.

Next steps

- Multi-Agent System — How agents collaborate, delegate, and share a workspace
- Agent Roles & Identity — Deep dive into role, goal, backstory, and autonomy
- Skills & Scheduling — Assigning skills and schedules to agents
- Channel Configuration — How to bind agents to specific Discord channels

title: Multi-Agent System
body:
Overview

Pantheon workspaces can run multiple agents, each with their own role, goal, skills, and Discord channel. Agents can delegate tasks to each other, creating a team of specialists that collaborates seamlessly.

Building a multi-agent team

Every team starts with one agent created during onboarding. Add more agents from the Agents page as your needs grow.

Example: Customer Support Team

| Agent | Role | Channel | Autonomy |
|-------|------|---------|----------|
| General Assistant | General operations assistant | All channels + DMs | Copilot |
| Support Lead | Customer support specialist | support-tickets | Copilot |
| Research Analyst | Research analyst | research | Assisted |

Example: Operations Team

| Agent | Role | Channel | Autonomy |
|-------|------|---------|----------|
| Executive Assistant | General operations assistant | All channels + DMs | Copilot |
| Operations Lead | Operations lead | operations | Autopilot |
| Research Analyst | Research analyst | research | Assisted |
| Comms Manager | Communications manager | comms | Copilot |

These are examples — you define the roles, goals, and channel assignments. There are no fixed configurations.

Start with one or two agents and add specialists as you identify specific needs. 2-4 agents is the practical sweet spot for most teams.

How delegation works

When agents have delegation enabled, they can route questions to the right specialist automatically.

The tools

Each agent with delegation enabled has access to two communication tools:

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| agentslist | Lists all other agents, their roles, and channel bindings |
| sessionssend | Sends a message to another agent and receives a response |

Example flow

1. A user asks the General Assistant in general: "Can you research our competitor's pricing?"
2. The General Assistant checks agentslist and sees the Research Analyst is available
3. It tells the user: "Let me hand this off to our research specialist..."
4. It uses sessionssend to pass the question to the Research Analyst
5. The Research Analyst processes the request and returns findings
6. The General Assistant relays the response in general

The user gets a specialist-quality answer without leaving their channel.

Delegation settings

Each agent has two delegation toggles:

| Setting | What it controls |
|---------|-----------------|
| Can delegate | This agent can send tasks to other agents |
| Can receive delegation | This agent accepts tasks from other agents |

Delegation is driven by the agent's role and goal. Agents learn which questions to handle themselves and which to pass along based on these fields.

If the target agent doesn't exist or delegation is disabled, the originating agent handles the question itself. You don't need to configure routing rules — agents figure it out from their role definitions.

Channel routing

Each agent can be bound to a specific Discord channel. The default agent handles all unbound channels and DMs.

| Configuration | What happens |
|--------------|-------------|
| Agent bound to support-tickets | Only responds in that channel |
| Default agent | Handles general, DMs, and any other unbound channels |
| No channel binding | Agent only responds to delegated tasks |

Every workspace must have exactly one default agent.

Creating agents

1. Go to Agents in the sidebar
2. Click + New Agent
3. Choose to start from a template or from scratch
4. Define the role, goal, backstory, and autonomy level
5. Optionally bind to a Discord channel
6. Add custom skills and schedules
7. Save

Agent templates pre-fill role, goal, and backstory to help you get started quickly. You can customize every field after selecting a template.

Practical tips

- One default agent is required. It catches everything that doesn't have a dedicated specialist.
- Use clear, non-overlapping roles. If two agents have similar goals, they may compete on the same questions.
- Enable delegation on the default agent. It should be able to route questions to specialists.
- Specialists can be receive-only. Set "Can delegate" to off for focused agents that shouldn't redirect questions elsewhere.
- Test with the Preview feature. Send test messages to see how routing and delegation work before going live.

Next steps

- Agent Roles & Identity — How to define role, goal, backstory, and autonomy
- Skills & Scheduling — Assign skills and schedules to agents
- Channel Configuration — Bind agents to specific Discord channels

title: Agent Roles & Identity
body:
The Role / Goal / Backstory Pattern

Every Pantheon agent is defined by three core identity fields. This pattern gives you precise control over how each agent behaves without relying on fixed presets.

Role

What is this agent?

A short description of the agent's function — think of it as a job title.

Examples:
- "Customer support specialist"
- "Research analyst"
- "Operations lead"
- "Content editor and proofreader"

Keep it under 200 characters. Be specific — "Sales development representative" is better than "Sales agent."

Goal

What should this agent accomplish?

The agent's primary objective. If this agent does its job well, what outcome do we see?

Examples:
- "Resolve customer questions quickly and accurately using our knowledge base"
- "Track open work, keep execution moving, and make sure nothing important stalls"
- "Investigate questions, synthesize findings, and provide clear recommendations with caveats"

Focus on outcomes, not process. A good goal helps the agent prioritize when it has multiple options.

Backstory (optional)

Personality, tone, and constraints.

Use it to define communication style, set boundaries, or provide important context.

Examples:
- "You are calm, structured, and concise. Summarize decisions clearly, surface blockers early, and avoid unnecessary complexity."
- "You are warm and helpful but never make up information. Always cite your sources."
- "You think in priorities, dependencies, and deadlines. You are direct and focused on turning ambiguity into next actions."

Include constraints ("never", "always") for important boundaries. Include tone guidance ("friendly", "formal", "direct"). Up to 2000 characters.

Autonomy Levels

Every agent has an autonomy level that controls how independently it operates.

L1 — Assisted

Always asks before taking any action. Presents options and waits for explicit approval.

Best for: High-stakes decisions, new agents you're still calibrating, situations where human oversight is critical.

L2 — Copilot (default)

Suggests actions and explains reasoning. Executes read-only operations automatically but asks before write operations. Proactively surfaces relevant information.

Best for: Most agents. Good balance of autonomy and oversight.

L3 — Autopilot

Takes actions independently based on its goal. Only asks when genuinely ambiguous or high-stakes. Reports results after execution.

Best for: Well-defined operational tasks, agents with clear boundaries, high-volume routine work.

Delegation

Agents can collaborate through delegation:

| Setting | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Can delegate | This agent can hand off tasks to other agents |
| Can receive delegation | This agent accepts tasks from other agents |

When delegation is enabled, agents use agentslist to discover teammates and sessionssend to pass messages. Users see a seamless experience.

Agent Templates

When creating a new agent, you can start from a template that pre-fills the role, goal, backstory, and autonomy level. Templates are starting points — you can customize every field after selecting one.

Available templates include:
- General Assistant — Flexible first agent for triage, coordination, and everyday requests (Copilot)
- Operations Lead — Execution tracking, SOP follow-through, and issue escalation (Autopilot)
- Research Analyst — Structured research, synthesis, and knowledge-heavy questions (Assisted)
- Customer Support — Inbound questions, resolution handoff, and service quality (Copilot)

You can also create agents entirely from scratch without a template.

Tips for Effective Agent Design

1. Start specific, broaden later. A narrow role with a clear goal performs better than a vague one.
2. Use backstory for constraints. If there are things the agent should never do, put them in the backstory.
3. Match autonomy to risk. Use L3 Autopilot for low-risk routine tasks; L1 Assisted for anything with consequences.
4. Test with real questions. Use the Preview feature to test how your agent responds before deploying.

What all agents share

Regardless of how you configure them, every Pantheon agent includes:

- Team context — Your team name, team goal, and timezone are embedded in the system prompt
- Security boundaries — Protection against prompt injection, credential leaks, and unauthorized modifications
- File creation — All agents can generate spreadsheets, CSVs, PDFs, charts, and other files as Discord attachments
- Inter-agent communication — All agents can discover teammates and delegate questions
- Knowledge access — Agents can reference uploaded knowledge files (shared or per-agent)

Next steps

- Multi-Agent System — How agents collaborate and delegate
- Skills & Scheduling — Assigning skills and schedules to agents
- Channel Configuration — Bind agents to specific Discord channels

title: Skills & Scheduling

headings.title: Example: Setting up a three-agent team

body:
Skills & Scheduling

Each agent in your Pantheon workspace can be assigned custom skills and its own set of scheduled messages. This lets you create focused specialists — a Support Lead with FAQ skills, a Research Analyst with web search, or an Operations Lead with daily standup schedules.

Custom skills

Skills are Markdown-based instructions (SKILL.md format) that teach agents how to handle specific tasks. All skills in Pantheon are custom — you create them for your specific use case.

Creating skills

There are three ways to create skills:

1. Skill Forge — Write a skill from scratch using the built-in Markdown editor
2. AI Generation — Describe what you want and Pantheon generates the skill using AI
3. Extension Marketplace — Install pre-built skills from the community

Assigning skills to agents

1. Go to Agents in the sidebar
2. Select the agent you want to configure
3. Under Skills, toggle each custom skill on or off
4. Click Save Changes

Each agent only has access to its assigned skills. A Research Analyst might have a "Web Search" skill, while a Support Lead has "FAQ Lookup" and "Ticket Triage" skills.

Create a skill once, then assign it to any combination of agents. A skill like "Company Knowledge Base" might be assigned to every agent, while "Competitor Analysis" is only assigned to the Research Analyst.

Schedules (automated messages)

Agents can send scheduled messages to their assigned Discord channel on a cron-based schedule.

How schedules work

Each schedule has:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Label | A human-readable name (e.g., "Daily Summary") |
| Cron expression | When the schedule runs (e.g., 0 8 1-5 for weekdays at 8 AM) |
| Message template | The prompt sent to the agent when the schedule fires |
| Enabled | Whether the schedule is currently active |

When a schedule fires, Pantheon sends the message template to the agent as if a user had typed it. The agent processes it using its role, goal, skills, and knowledge, then posts the response to its Discord channel.

Built-in schedule templates

Pantheon provides generic schedule templates to get you started:

| Template | Default schedule | What it does |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| Daily Summary | Weekdays at 8:00 AM | Agent reviews the day's priorities and posts a summary |
| Weekly Report | Mondays at 9:00 AM | Agent compiles a weekly overview |
| Morning Standup | Daily at 7:30 AM | Agent posts a structured standup prompt |

These are starting points — you can customize the schedule, message template, and everything else.

Creating custom schedules

1. Go to Agents in the sidebar
2. Select an agent
3. Under Schedules, click + Add Schedule
4. Set the label, cron expression, and message template
5. Save

Schedules run in the agent's timezone (inherited from the team timezone set during onboarding).

Scheduled messages are posted to the agent's bound Discord channel. If the agent has no channel binding, the schedule posts to the default channel.

Channel bindings

Each agent can be bound to a specific Discord channel:

- Bound channels — Only the assigned agent responds in that channel
- Unbound channels — The default agent handles messages
- DMs — The default agent always handles direct messages

This lets you create dedicated channels: support-tickets for the Support Lead, research for the Research Analyst, etc.

Every workspace must have exactly one default agent. It handles DMs and any channel not bound to a specific agent.

Example: Setting up a three-agent team

1. Create your custom skills.

Go to Settings > Skills and create the skills your agents will need (e.g., "FAQ Lookup", "Ticket Triage", "Web Search").

2. Create Agent 1: General Assistant (default).

- Role: General operations assistant
- Goal: Keep the team organized and route work to the right place
- Skills: FAQ Lookup
- Channel: (none — handles unbound channels and DMs)
- Default: Yes

3. Create Agent 2: Support Lead.

- Role: Customer support specialist
- Goal: Resolve customer questions quickly and accurately
- Skills: FAQ Lookup, Ticket Triage
- Schedule: Daily Summary at 8:00 AM
- Channel: support-tickets
- Default: No

4. Create Agent 3: Research Analyst.

- Role: Research analyst
- Goal: Investigate questions and provide clear recommendations
- Skills: Web Search
- Schedule: Weekly Report on Mondays
- Channel: research
- Default: No

5. Save and test.

Each agent responds in their designated channel. The General Assistant handles everything else and delegates specialist questions when appropriate.

Next steps

- Custom Skills — Deep dive into creating and managing skills
- Agent Roles & Identity — Configuring role, goal, backstory, and autonomy
- Channel Configuration — Discord channel setup and binding

title: Billing & Usage

headings.title: What's included in the base subscription

body:
How billing works

Pantheon uses a straightforward pricing model with two components:

1. Monthly base subscription — A fixed $50/month fee that covers your AI team and platform access
2. Metered API usage — Billed at cost based on the actual AI model tokens your agents consume

This structure keeps the base price low while ensuring you only pay for what you use.

What's included in the base subscription

Your $50/month subscription includes:

- Unlimited AI agents with custom roles, goals, and backstories
- Custom skills (create your own or install from the marketplace)
- Knowledge file uploads (PDF, DOCX, TXT, Markdown)
- Multi-agent collaboration with delegation
- Email integration
- Workflow builder
- Memory vault
- Access to the extension marketplace
- Dashboard with usage analytics
- Encrypted configuration storage

What counts as API usage

Every time your AI agents process a message — whether answering a question, running a scheduled task, or handling a delegated request — they use tokens from the underlying AI model (Claude by Anthropic).

Token usage varies by task:

- Simple question/answer: 500–1,000 tokens
- Research and synthesis: 2,000–4,000 tokens
- Document analysis: 3,000–5,000 tokens
- Scheduled daily summary: 1,500–3,000 tokens

For a typical team, metered usage runs $5–15/month depending on how actively you use the agents.

Managing your billing

All billing is managed through Settings > Billing in your Pantheon dashboard. From there you can:

- View your current plan and billing period
- See your metered usage breakdown
- Update your payment method
- Access invoices and billing history
- Cancel your subscription

Payments are processed securely through Stripe. Pantheon never stores your credit card information directly.

title: Metered Usage
body:
How metering works

Every interaction with your AI agent consumes tokens -- the unit of measurement for AI model usage. Pantheon tracks token usage daily and bills it as a separate line item on top of your $50/month subscription.

What are tokens?

Tokens are the building blocks of AI text processing. Roughly:

- 1 token 4 characters of English text
- 100 tokens 75 words
- A typical question and answer uses 500--2,000 tokens

Both the message you send and the response your agent generates count toward token usage.

Usage by task

Different tasks consume different amounts of tokens:

| Task | Typical Token Usage |
|------|-------------------|
| Simple question/answer | 500--1,000 |
| Data lookup or retrieval | 1,500--2,500 |
| Multi-source comparison | 2,000--4,000 |
| Report generation | 1,000--2,000 |
| Scheduled briefing | 1,500--3,000 |
| Document analysis | 1,000--2,000 |
| Image analysis (OCR) | 3,000--5,000 |
| Complex multi-step task | 2,000--4,000 |

Typical monthly costs

For most teams, metered API usage runs $5--15/month:

| Usage Level | Description | Estimated Cost |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| Light | A few questions per day, automated briefings | $5--8/month |
| Moderate | Regular queries, report generation, scheduled tasks | $8--12/month |
| Heavy | Frequent interactions, multiple agents, image processing | $12--20/month |

Tracking your usage

Dashboard

Your Pantheon dashboard shows real-time usage analytics:

- Daily token consumption -- Bar chart showing tokens used per day
- Cost breakdown -- Running total for the current billing period
- Per-model breakdown -- Token usage split by AI model
- Trend lines -- See how your usage changes over time

Usage table

The apiusage table tracks daily metering per model:

- Date
- Model used
- Input tokens consumed
- Output tokens generated
- Estimated cost

Access your usage data anytime from Settings > Usage or the main dashboard.

Cost transparency

Pantheon bills API usage at cost -- we don't mark up token prices. You pay exactly what the underlying AI model charges, with full visibility into your usage.

The AI model powering your agents is Claude by Anthropic. Current token pricing is displayed in your usage dashboard and updated if model pricing changes.

Reducing usage

If you want to keep costs down:

- Disable unused cron jobs -- Automated briefings run on schedule even if you're not reading them
- Use fewer agents -- Each agent in a multi-agent setup consumes tokens independently
- Be specific -- Clear, direct questions use fewer tokens than vague ones
- Use text input instead of images when possible -- image processing uses significantly more tokens

title: Spending Cap

headings.title: What happens when the cap is reached

body:
Spending Cap

The spending cap lets you set a maximum monthly budget for metered AI usage. Once your agents reach the cap, they pause automatically -- protecting you from unexpected charges.

Setting a spending cap

1. Go to Settings > Billing (or Settings > Spending Cap)
2. Enter your monthly spending limit ($1--$1,000)
3. Toggle Auto-pause at cap to control whether your agents stop when the limit is reached
4. Optionally set an alert email to receive spending notifications
5. Click Save

The cap applies to metered API usage only -- it does not affect your $50/month base subscription.

Spending alerts

Pantheon sends alerts at three thresholds:

| Threshold | Severity | Notification |
|---|---|---|
| 50% of cap | Info | Dashboard notification |
| 80% of cap | Warning | Dashboard + email alert |
| 100% of cap | Critical | Dashboard + email alert |

Pantheon also detects spending anomalies -- if today's usage is more than 3x your daily average (and over $0.50), you'll receive a warning.

Spending is checked automatically every 4 hours.

What happens when the cap is reached

When auto-pause is enabled and your metered usage reaches the spending cap:

- Your agents stop responding to new messages in Discord
- Scheduled jobs are paused
- Your dashboard shows a clear indicator that the cap is active

When the spending cap is reached with auto-pause enabled, your agents will not respond until the next billing period starts or you increase the cap. Make sure your cap is set high enough for your normal usage.

Resuming after a pause

Your agents resume automatically when you:

- Increase the cap above your current month's spending -- the pause clears immediately
- Remove the cap -- set the value to $0 or clear the field
- Wait for the next billing period -- usage resets at the start of each month

Recommended cap levels

| Usage Level | Recommended Cap |
|---|---|
| Light (a few questions/day) | $15--20/month |
| Moderate (regular daily use) | $25--30/month |
| Heavy (frequent use, multiple agents, image processing) | $40--50/month |

If you're unsure, start with a $30 cap and adjust based on your actual usage.

Monitoring usage

Track your spending in real time from the dashboard:

- Current period usage -- How much you've spent so far this billing period
- Daily breakdown -- Token usage and cost per day
- Cap utilization -- Progress bar showing how close you are to your cap

See Metered Usage for details on what drives costs and how to reduce usage.

What's next

- Metered Usage -- Understand token costs and usage patterns
- Subscription -- Manage your base subscription

title: Subscription
body:
Plan details

Pantheon offers a single subscription plan:

| Feature | Details |
|---------|---------|
| Monthly price | $50.00 |
| Billing cycle | Monthly |
| Payment processor | Stripe |
| Cancellation | Anytime, effective end of billing period |

What you get

Your subscription activates your Pantheon workspace, which includes:

- Platform tools -- Memory vault, knowledge files, email integration, custom skills
- Multi-agent support -- Up to 4 specialized agents
- Extension marketplace -- Access to additional capabilities
- Dashboard -- Usage analytics, settings management
- Automated scheduling -- Cron jobs for recurring briefings and reports

Activating your subscription

During onboarding, you'll be redirected to Stripe's secure checkout:

1. Click "Subscribe" on the activation prompt
2. Enter your payment information on the Stripe checkout page
3. Complete the purchase
4. You're redirected back to Pantheon to continue onboarding

All payment processing is handled by Stripe. Pantheon never sees or stores your credit card number. Stripe is PCI Level 1 certified -- the highest level of payment security.

Managing your subscription

Access your subscription settings at Settings > Billing:

Update payment method

Click "Manage Billing" to open the Stripe customer portal where you can update your card on file, switch payment methods, or add a backup payment method.

View invoices

The Stripe portal shows your complete billing history with downloadable PDF invoices for each billing period.

Cancel your subscription

You can cancel anytime from the Stripe portal. Your workspace will remain active until the end of your current billing period. After that:

- Your AI agents go offline
- Your data (team profile, configuration, knowledge files) is retained for 30 days
- You can reactivate within 30 days to restore everything

After 30 days of cancellation, your workspace data may be permanently deleted. Export any important data before canceling.

title: Channel Configuration
body:
Overview

Channel configuration lets you control which AI agent responds in which Discord channel. This is how you keep conversations organized -- different topics go to different agents, and your default agent handles everything else.

If you're running a single agent (the default), it responds in all channels and DMs. Channel bindings only matter when you have multiple agents set up.

How channel bindings work

Each agent in your Pantheon workspace can be bound to one or more specific Discord channels. When a message comes in on a bound channel, only the assigned agent responds.

Here are the key rules:

- Bound channels -- Only the assigned agent responds in that channel
- Unbound channels -- The default agent handles any channel without an explicit binding
- Direct messages -- Always handled by the default agent
- One agent per channel -- Each channel can only have one agent bound to it

Every Pantheon workspace must have exactly one agent marked as the default. This agent is the catch-all -- it handles DMs and any channel that isn't explicitly bound to another agent.

Example setup

Here's a common three-agent configuration with channel bindings:

| Discord Channel | Bound Agent | Role | What it handles |
|----------------|-------------|------|-----------------|
| general | Team Assistant (default) | General | Daily questions, broad topics, delegation |
| research | Research Agent | Research | Deep dives, data analysis, report generation |
| support | Team Assistant (default) | General | Unbound channel, handled by default agent |
| tasks | Task Agent | Operations | Task tracking, project updates, scheduling |
| DMs | Team Assistant (default) | General | Private questions, always default agent |

In this example, research and tasks are bound to specialist agents. general and support have no explicit binding, so the default Team Assistant handles them.

You don't need to bind every channel on day one. Start with your default agent handling everything, then add specialist agents and channel bindings as you see which topics come up most.

Configuring channel bindings

From the dashboard

1. Go to Settings > Channels in your Pantheon dashboard
2. You'll see a list of your Discord server's channels
3. For each channel, select which agent should handle it from the dropdown
4. Channels left as "Default" will be handled by your default agent
5. Click Save

Changes apply to your agents immediately.

When creating an agent

You can also assign a channel when creating or editing an agent:

1. Go to Settings > Agents
2. Create a new agent or edit an existing one
3. Under Channel Binding, select a Discord channel
4. Save the agent

How agents handle cross-channel requests

If someone asks a question in general that would be better handled by the Research agent in research, the default agent can delegate the question. With inter-agent communication enabled, agents can:

1. Recognize that a question falls outside their expertise
2. Use agentslist to see which specialists are available
3. Use sessionssend to pass the question to the right agent
4. Inform the user that they're delegating to the specialist

The response comes back in the same channel where the question was asked. The user doesn't need to switch channels.

You don't need to configure delegation rules. Each agent's role and goal includes guidance on when to defer to other agents. Agents know to pass questions to the most relevant specialist based on their defined expertise.

Channel configuration and security

Your Discord channel configuration is encrypted at rest in Pantheon's database. Channel IDs and the mapping between channels and agents are stored using AES encryption, the same standard used for all sensitive data.

The configuration is decrypted at runtime when your agents need to connect to Discord.

Common patterns

Single agent (default)

No channel configuration needed. Your one agent handles everything everywhere.

Two agents: General + Research

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| research | Research Agent |
| Everything else | Team Assistant (default) |

Three agents: General + Research + Tasks

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| research | Research Agent |
| tasks | Task Agent |
| Everything else | Team Assistant (default) |

Four agents: Full Team

| Channel | Agent |
|---------|-------|
| research | Research Agent |
| reports | Reports Agent |
| tasks | Task Agent |
| Everything else | Team Assistant (default) |

Next steps

- AI Agents -- Learn about multi-agent setups
- Multi-Agent Setups -- Pre-built 2, 3, and 4-agent configurations

title: Discord Integration
body:
Why Discord?

Pantheon delivers your AI agents through Discord because it's the best fit for how teams actually work:

- Mobile-first -- Discord runs on your phone, tablet, and desktop. Ask your agents questions from anywhere.
- Already familiar -- Many teams already use Discord for communication. No new app to learn.
- Multi-channel -- Organize conversations by topic. Put different workflows in different channels.
- Threaded conversations -- Keep discussions organized without losing context. Ask a follow-up question in a thread without cluttering the main channel.
- File sharing built in -- Your agents can send spreadsheets, charts, and PDFs directly in the conversation. Download files right to your phone.
- Notifications you control -- Set up push notifications for the channels that matter. Get alerted for important updates but mute channels on weekends.

One server, one workspace

Each Pantheon workspace connects to one Discord server. When you sign up and complete onboarding, the Pantheon bot joins your server and is ready to respond to messages.

If you don't already have a Discord server, you'll create one during setup. If you have an existing server for your team, you can add the Pantheon bot to it.

What you can configure

Pantheon gives you control over how your agents operate within Discord:

Bot setup

Add the Pantheon bot to your Discord server during onboarding with a single click. No bot tokens or Developer Portal steps needed. See the Server Setup guide for step-by-step instructions.

Channel configuration

Map specific AI agents to specific Discord channels. For example, bind your Research agent to research and your Support agent to support. Any channel without an explicit binding is handled by your default agent. See Channel Configuration for details.

Agent bindings

If you're running a multi-agent setup, each agent can be assigned to one or more channels. This keeps conversations focused -- each agent only responds in its assigned channels, and your default agent covers everything else plus DMs.

Learn more about agents in the AI Agents section.

How it works in practice

Once your workspace is set up, using Pantheon is as simple as typing a message in Discord:

Your agent responds in the same channel, with formatted tables, data citations, and timestamps. It can also generate and attach files -- spreadsheets, charts, PDFs -- directly in the conversation.

Anything you can do from the Discord desktop app, you can do from the mobile app. Pantheon responds the same way regardless of how you connect.

Next steps

- Server Setup -- Add the Pantheon bot to your Discord server
- Channel Configuration -- Map agents to specific channels
- AI Agents -- Learn about multi-agent setups and agent identities

title: Server Setup

headings.title: Step 2: Add Pantheon to your Discord server
How to find your Server ID
Can't add the bot to my server

body:
Overview

This guide walks you through creating a Discord server and connecting it to Pantheon. The entire process takes about 5 minutes.

You'll need:

- A Discord account (free at discord.com)
- Access to your Pantheon onboarding wizard or Settings page

Step 1: Create a Discord server

If you already have a Discord server for your team, skip to Step 2.

1. Open Discord (desktop app or web)
2. Click the + button in the server list on the left sidebar
3. Select Create My Own
4. Choose For me and my friends (or For a club or community if you prefer)
5. Name your server -- something like "Acme Team" or "My Workspace"
6. Click Create

Your server is ready. You can customize it later with channels, roles, and permissions.

Consider creating channels that match your workflow: general, research, reports, tasks. You can bind different AI agents to each channel later.

Step 2: Add Pantheon to your Discord server

Pantheon uses a single shared bot -- you don't need to create your own bot application or manage any tokens.

During onboarding

On the Discord Connection step of the onboarding wizard, click Add Pantheon to Discord. This opens a standard Discord OAuth authorization page where you select your server and grant the bot the permissions it needs.

Alternatively, you can paste your Discord Server ID directly if you prefer.

After onboarding

If you need to connect a different server later:

1. Go to Settings > Discord in your Pantheon dashboard
2. Click Add Pantheon to Discord or enter your Server ID
3. Click Save

How to find your Server ID

If you're pasting a Server ID manually:

1. Open Discord and right-click your server name
2. Click Copy Server ID (you may need to enable Developer Mode in Discord settings first: User Settings > Advanced > Developer Mode)
3. Paste the ID into Pantheon

Bot permissions

The Pantheon bot requests these permissions when you add it to your server:

| Permission | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| Send Messages | Respond to your questions and post updates |
| Read Message History | Provide context in threaded conversations |
| Embed Links | Format responses with rich embeds (tables, cards) |
| Attach Files | Send spreadsheets, charts, and PDFs |
| Read Messages / View Channels | See messages in the channels it's assigned to |

These are the minimum permissions needed. Pantheon does not request administrator access or any permissions beyond what's listed above.

Once you complete onboarding, your agent is ready. The Pantheon bot will appear online in your Discord server. Send it a message to verify everything is working.

Troubleshooting

Bot shows offline

- Verify your workspace is active on the Pantheon dashboard
- Check that the Pantheon bot hasn't been kicked from the server -- re-add it from Settings > Discord

Bot doesn't respond to messages

- Make sure the bot has Read Messages/View Channels and Send Messages permissions in the channel you're testing
- If using channel bindings, make sure the channel you're in is bound to an agent or is handled by the default agent
- Try messaging the bot in a DM to rule out channel permission issues

Can't add the bot to my server

- You need the Manage Server permission in Discord to add bots. Ask a server admin if you don't have this permission.
- If the authorization page shows an error, try logging out of Discord in your browser and logging back in.

Next steps

- Channel Configuration -- Map agents to specific Discord channels
- AI Agents -- Set up multiple agents with different roles

title: Extensions
body:
Extensions

The Pantheon extension system lets you expand your AI assistant's capabilities beyond the three built-in skills. Extensions are installable packages that add new skills, plugins, connectors, MCP servers, and tool packs to your workspace.

Extension types

Extensions come in five types, each serving a different purpose:

| Type | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| skill | Adds a new capability or workflow to your assistant | A lead qualification skill, a compliance checker |
| plugin | Modifies or enhances existing assistant behavior | A response formatter, a conversation logger |
| connector | Integrates with an external service or API | A CRM bridge, a data feed connector |
| mcpserver | Adds a new MCP server for tool access | A custom database connector, a cloud storage bridge |
| toolpack | Bundles multiple related tools together | A project planning toolkit, a financial analysis suite |

Source types

Extensions can come from five different sources. Each source type has different trust and verification characteristics.

| Source | Description | Supports verification |
|---|---|---|
| internal | First-party extensions built and maintained by Pantheon | No (implicitly trusted) |
| local | Extensions bundled directly with the platform | No (implicitly trusted) |
| clawhub | Third-party packages hosted on the Pantheon marketplace | Yes |
| npm | Extensions published to the Node package registry | Yes |
| git | Extensions hosted in Git repositories, pinned by catalog reference | Yes |

Your account has a trust policy that determines which source types are allowed and which require verification before installation. By default, all sources are allowed, and npm, git, and clawhub sources require verified status. See Installing Extensions for details.

How extensions work

Extensions are managed through a catalog-and-installation model:

1. Catalog -- Pantheon maintains a catalog of available extensions. Each catalog entry has a slug, type, source, description, and one or more versioned releases.
2. Installation -- When you install an extension, Pantheon creates an installation record tied to your customer account and workspace. The installation tracks which version is active and the current health status.
3. Operations -- Install, upgrade, rollback, and remove actions are queued as operations. Each operation is tracked with a status (pending, inprogress, completed, failed, canceled) so you can monitor progress.
4. Health monitoring -- Installed extensions are continuously monitored. Each installation reports a health status: healthy, degraded, unhealthy, or unknown.

Versioned installations

Every extension in the catalog has one or more published versions. When you install an extension, you get the latest version by default. Each version includes:

- A semantic version number
- Release notes describing what changed
- A manifest with configuration and dependency information
- An optional SHA-256 checksum for integrity verification

You can pin an installation to a specific version to prevent automatic upgrades, or leave it unpinned to receive new versions through the rollout system.

Managing extensions

Extensions are managed through the Pantheon dashboard at Settings > Extensions. The panel provides three sections:

- Trust Policy -- Configure which source types are allowed and which require verification.
- Installed -- View your active installations with status, health, and version info. Queue rollbacks if needed.
- Catalog -- Browse available extensions, see verification status, and install or upgrade with one click.

If an extension update causes problems, you can queue a rollback from the Installed section to revert to the previous version.

Extension lifecycle

An extension installation moves through the following statuses over its lifetime:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| pending | Installation or upgrade has been queued but not yet started |
| installing | The operation is actively running |
| installed | The extension is active and operational |
| failed | The installation or upgrade encountered an error |
| rollbackpending | A rollback has been queued |
| rolledback | Successfully reverted to a previous version |
| removed | The extension has been uninstalled |

What's next

- Marketplace -- Browse and discover available extensions
- Installing Extensions -- Trust policies, installation flow, health monitoring, and rollouts
- MCP Servers -- Built-in tool servers for filesystem, database, and memory access

title: Installing Extensions
body:
Installing Extensions

This guide covers the complete extension installation workflow: configuring your trust policy, installing extensions from the catalog, monitoring health, and understanding the staged rollout system for updates.

Trust policies

Every Pantheon account has an extension trust policy that controls which extensions can be installed. The trust policy has two components:

Allowed source types

The allowedsourcetypes list determines which source types you permit at all. By default, all five source types are allowed:

- internal -- Pantheon first-party extensions
- local -- Extensions bundled with the platform
- clawhub -- Pantheon marketplace packages
- npm -- Node package registry packages
- git -- Git repository extensions

You must keep at least one source type allowed at all times.

Verification requirements

The requireverifiedsourcetypes list determines which allowed sources must be verified before installation. By default, three source types require verification:

- npm
- git
- clawhub

Internal and local sources do not support verification -- they are implicitly trusted because Pantheon or your deployment controls them.

Out of the box, your trust policy allows all source types and requires verification for npm, git, and clawhub. This provides a balanced security posture: first-party and local extensions are always trusted, while third-party sources must be verified.

Configuring your trust policy

1. Go to Settings > Extensions.
2. In the Trust Policy section, toggle each source type on or off under the Allowed column.
3. For source types that support verification (clawhub, npm, git), toggle Require verified on or off.
4. Click Save Policy.

The policy takes effect immediately for all future installation requests.

| Source type | Can be allowed | Can require verification |
|---|---|---|
| internal | Yes | No (implicitly trusted) |
| local | Yes | No (implicitly trusted) |
| clawhub | Yes | Yes |
| npm | Yes | Yes |
| git | Yes | Yes |

If you set a source type to require verification but then disallow that source entirely, the verification requirement is automatically removed. Verified-required source types must also be in your allowed list.

How trust evaluation works

When you attempt to install an extension, Pantheon evaluates it against your trust policy in two steps:

1. Source type check -- Is the extension's source type in your allowedsourcetypes? If not, the install is blocked.
2. Verification check -- Is the source type in your requireverifiedsourcetypes? If yes, is the extension marked as verified? If it requires verification and is not verified, the install is blocked.

If both checks pass, the installation proceeds.

Installation flow

Installing an extension from the catalog follows this sequence:

Step 1: Select an extension

Browse the Marketplace and find the extension you want to install. Click Install on the extension card.

Step 2: Trust policy check

Pantheon automatically evaluates the extension against your trust policy. If the extension is blocked, you receive an error message explaining the reason (e.g., "source type 'npm' is blocked by trust policy" or "extension is unverified for source type 'clawhub'").

Step 3: Version resolution

Pantheon resolves the target version. By default, this is the latest published version. If you are upgrading an existing installation, the latest version that is newer than your current version is selected.

You can optionally pin an installation to a specific version. Pinned installations do not receive automatic upgrades through the rollout system.

Step 4: Operation queued

Pantheon creates an installation record (or updates an existing one) and queues an operation. The operation tracks the installation's progress:

| Operation field | Description |
|---|---|
| operationtype | install for new installations, upgrade for existing ones |
| scopetype | workspace if tied to a specific workspace, customer if account-wide |
| status | Starts as pending, progresses to inprogress, then completed or failed |

Step 5: Execution

The queued operation is picked up by Pantheon's operation processor. It installs the extension package, configures it for your workspace, and updates the installation status.

Step 6: Confirmation

Once complete, the installation status changes to installed and the extension is active in your workspace. The health monitor begins tracking the extension's status.

Extension installations are processed asynchronously. After clicking Install, you can navigate away from the page. Check back on the Installed section to see the current status.

Health monitoring

Every installed extension is monitored for health. The health status appears as a badge next to each installation in the Installed section.

| Health status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| unknown | Health has not been evaluated yet (typical right after install) |
| healthy | The extension is operating normally |
| degraded | The extension is experiencing intermittent issues |
| unhealthy | The extension is failing consistently |

Health status is updated automatically. If an extension becomes unhealthy, you can queue a rollback to revert to the previous version.

Viewing health details

Each installed extension card shows:

- Install status -- Current installation state (installed, pending, failed, etc.)
- Health status -- Current health evaluation
- Last error -- If the extension has encountered an error, the most recent error message is displayed
- Version -- The currently installed version number
- Last updated -- When the installation was last modified

Rollbacks

If an extension update causes problems, you can revert to the previous version.

1. Go to Settings > Extensions.
2. Find the extension in the Installed section.
3. Click Queue Rollback.

Pantheon queues a rollback operation that reverts the installation to the previous version. The installation status changes to rollbackpending and then to rolledback once complete.

A rollback changes the active version but does not delete the newer version from the catalog. You can upgrade again later when a fix is available.

Rollout system

When extension authors publish new versions, Pantheon uses a staged rollout system to deploy updates safely across all installations. This prevents a bad update from affecting every customer at once.

Rollout rings

Updates are deployed in three rings, each progressively larger:

| Ring | Default batch size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| canary | 1 instance | Early validation on a single installation |
| standard | 10 instances | Broader validation across a small group |
| delayed | 100 instances | Full rollout to remaining installations |

The rollout advances from canary to standard to delayed, pausing between rings to evaluate health metrics.

Gate policy

Between each ring, Pantheon evaluates the update against health thresholds called the gate policy. If any threshold is exceeded, the rollout is halted and affected installations are rolled back.

| Metric | Default threshold | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Max failure rate | 10% | Maximum percentage of installations that can fail |
| Max P95 latency | 10,000 ms (10s) | Maximum 95th percentile response latency |
| Max timeout rate | 5% | Maximum percentage of installations that time out |
| Max hard error rate | 3% | Maximum percentage of installations with unrecoverable errors |

If the gate policy thresholds are exceeded during a rollout, Pantheon automatically halts the rollout and rolls back affected installations. This protects your workspace from bad updates.

Rollout statuses

A rollout progresses through these statuses:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| pending | Rollout has been created but not started |
| inprogress | Currently deploying to the active ring |
| paused | Manually paused by an administrator |
| completed | All rings deployed successfully |
| failed | A ring failed and the rollout was stopped |
| canceled | Manually canceled before completion |
| halted | Automatically stopped due to gate policy violation |

Per-target tracking

Each installation targeted by a rollout has its own tracking record with:

- Ring assignment -- Which ring the installation belongs to
- Status -- pending, inprogress, completed, failed, skipped, or rolledback
- Attempt count -- How many times the update has been attempted
- Latency -- Measured response latency in milliseconds
- Timeout count -- Number of timeout events
- Hard error count -- Number of unrecoverable errors
- Error message -- Details if the target failed

Pinning versions

If you want to stay on a specific version and opt out of automatic rollouts:

1. Install the extension and note the version number.
2. The installation's pinnedversion field locks it to that version.

Pinned installations are skipped by the rollout system. To receive updates again, install the latest version manually, which clears the pin.

What's next

- Marketplace -- Browse and discover available extensions
- Extensions Overview -- Extension types, sources, and lifecycle
- Skills -- Built-in capabilities that come with every workspace
- MCP Servers -- Tool servers for filesystem, database, and memory access

title: Marketplace
body:
Marketplace

The Pantheon extension marketplace is the catalog of all available extensions you can add to your AI assistant. Browse by type, filter by source, and check verification status before installing.

Browsing the catalog

The extension catalog is accessible from Settings > Extensions in your Pantheon dashboard. The Catalog section at the bottom of the page displays all active extensions with their key details.

Each extension entry in the catalog shows:

- Display name -- The human-readable name of the extension.
- Slug -- A unique machine-readable identifier (e.g., project-planning-toolkit).
- Type -- The extension kind: skill, plugin, connector, mcpserver, or toolpack.
- Source type -- Where the extension comes from: internal, local, clawhub, npm, or git.
- Latest version -- The most recently published version number, if available.
- Verified status -- Whether the extension has been verified for its source type.

Verified extensions display a green verified badge. Unverified extensions display an amber unverified badge.

Filtering extensions

The catalog supports several filters to help you find what you need.

By type

Filter by extension type to narrow results to a specific category:

| Filter value | Shows |
|---|---|
| skill | Extensions that add new assistant capabilities |
| plugin | Extensions that modify or enhance existing behavior |
| connector | Extensions that integrate with external services |
| mcpserver | Extensions that add new MCP tool servers |
| toolpack | Extensions that bundle multiple related tools |

By source

Filter by source type to see extensions from a specific origin:

| Filter value | Description |
|---|---|
| internal | First-party extensions managed by Pantheon |
| local | Extensions bundled directly with the platform |
| clawhub | Third-party packages from the Pantheon marketplace |
| npm | Node package registry sources |
| git | Git repositories pinned by catalog reference |

By verification status

Toggle the verified filter to show only extensions that have passed verification for their source type. This is useful when your trust policy requires verification for certain sources.

By search

Use the search field to find extensions by name or slug. The search matches against the display name and slug fields.

Pagination

The catalog returns up to 20 items per page by default. You can request up to 100 items per page through the API. The dashboard handles pagination automatically, displaying results in a scrollable list.

The marketplace only displays extensions that have been marked as active in the catalog. Deprecated or deactivated extensions are hidden from the browsing view.

Source types explained

Understanding where an extension comes from helps you evaluate whether to trust and install it.

Internal

Internal extensions are built and maintained by the Pantheon team. They are first-party packages that ship with the platform and are implicitly trusted. Internal extensions do not require verification because Pantheon controls the source code and release process.

Local

Local extensions are bundled directly with the Pantheon platform. These are typically custom extensions that have been built specifically for your deployment. Like internal extensions, they are implicitly trusted because Pantheon controls the source.

ClawHub

ClawHub is the Pantheon-hosted marketplace for third-party extensions. Developers can publish extension packages to ClawHub, where they go through a review and verification process. ClawHub extensions support verification -- you can require that only verified ClawHub packages are installable through your trust policy.

ClawHub extensions go through a review process before being listed in the catalog. If you want third-party capabilities with an extra layer of trust, prioritize ClawHub-sourced extensions.

NPM

NPM extensions are published to the Node package registry. These are standard npm packages that implement the Pantheon extension interface. NPM sources support verification -- the catalog tracks whether a given npm package has been verified by the Pantheon team.

Git

Git extensions are hosted in Git repositories and pinned by a catalog reference (commit hash, tag, or branch). This source type is common for extensions in active development or extensions maintained by a specific organization. Git sources support verification.

Installing from the catalog

Each extension in the catalog has an Install button (or Queue Upgrade if you already have it installed). Clicking the button queues an installation operation.

Before the installation proceeds, Pantheon checks your trust policy:

1. Is the extension's source type in your allowedsourcetypes list?
2. If the source type is in your requireverifiedsourcetypes list, is the extension verified?

If either check fails, the installation is blocked and you receive an error message explaining why.

If you change your trust policy to disallow a source type, you will not be able to install new extensions from that source. Existing installations are not automatically removed, but you will not be able to upgrade them.

For the full installation workflow, trust policy configuration, and health monitoring details, see Installing Extensions.

What's next

- Installing Extensions -- Trust policies, installation flow, health monitoring, and staged rollouts
- Extensions Overview -- Extension types, lifecycle, and versioning
- MCP Servers -- Built-in tool servers available without the marketplace

title: Account Setup
body:
Magic link authentication

Pantheon uses magic link authentication powered by Supabase Auth. There are no passwords to remember or manage.

How it works

1. Enter your email address on the login or signup page
2. Check your inbox for an email from Pantheon
3. Click the login link in the email
4. You're signed in — the link is single-use and expires after a short time

Your session stays active as you use Pantheon. The system automatically refreshes your session in the background, so you won't be logged out during active use.

Troubleshooting login

If you don't receive the magic link email:

- Check your spam, junk, or promotions folder
- Make sure you entered the correct email address
- Wait a minute and try again — there's a short cooldown between requests
- If using a corporate email, check with your IT team about email filtering

Activating your subscription

After your first login, you'll need to activate a subscription before you can use Pantheon. The subscription is managed through Stripe.

Subscription details

| Item | Cost |
|------|------|
| Monthly base subscription | $50.00/month |
| Metered API usage | Billed at cost |

The base subscription covers platform access and your AI assistant. API usage (the AI model calls your assistant makes) is metered daily and billed separately based on actual token usage.

Checkout flow

1. Click "Subscribe" on the onboarding prompt
2. You'll be redirected to a Stripe checkout page
3. Enter your payment information
4. Complete the purchase
5. You'll be redirected back to Pantheon to begin onboarding

Managing your subscription

After setup, you can manage your subscription at any time from Settings > Billing:

- View your current plan and usage
- Update payment method
- Access billing history and invoices
- Cancel or modify your subscription

The billing portal is provided by Stripe and handles all payment processing securely.

Account security

- All sessions are managed server-side with secure HTTP-only cookies
- API routes validate authentication on every request
- Row-Level Security (RLS) ensures you can only access your own data
- Admin operations require a separate admin role

title: Welcome to Pantheon
body:
What is Pantheon?

Pantheon gives your team a dedicated AI agent system that lives in your Discord server. Ask your agents to research topics, draft content, pull data, automate tasks, and more -- all in plain English, right from your phone or desktop.

Each Pantheon workspace is a fully configured multi-agent system powered by Claude, Anthropic's AI model. You get real-time access to the capabilities that matter to your team.

Who is it for?

Pantheon is designed for teams and organizations that want AI-powered automation delivered through Discord:

- Small businesses looking for an always-available AI assistant
- Operations teams that need data retrieval and reporting
- Remote teams that already collaborate on Discord
- Agencies and consultants managing multiple workflows
- Anyone who wants a customizable multi-agent AI system without the infrastructure headaches

Whether you're a small team of 3 or a growing organization, Pantheon scales to your needs.

What can it do?

Pantheon provides a flexible platform for building and deploying AI agents:

- Custom Skills -- Create specialized skills tailored to your team's workflows. Write them in Markdown or generate them with AI assistance.
- Knowledge Files -- Upload documents (PDFs, Word docs, text files) that your agents can reference when answering questions.
- Memory Vault -- Persistent memory across conversations so your agents remember important facts, preferences, and decisions.
- Integrations -- Connect to Google Sheets, Gmail, Calendar, Slack, and 800+ third-party services through Composio.

Beyond the built-in tools, the extension marketplace lets you add more capabilities as they become available.

How does it work?

1. Sign up and activate your $50/month subscription
2. Complete onboarding -- name your team, configure your first agent, and connect your Discord server
3. Your workspace is created with your team configuration
4. Start chatting in Discord -- ask questions, run tasks, and interact with your agents

Your agents are always available. Scheduled messages and automated briefings can be configured to run on any cadence.

Next steps

Ready to get started? Head to the Quick Start guide for the full walkthrough.

title: Quick Start
body:
Overview

This guide walks you through the entire setup process. By the end, you'll have a working AI agent in your Discord server ready to help your team.

Step 1: Create your account

Go to the Pantheon signup page and enter your email address. We use magic link authentication -- no password required. Check your inbox for the login link and click it to access your dashboard.

Magic link emails occasionally land in spam or promotions folders. The email comes from Pantheon and contains a one-time login link.

Step 2: Activate your subscription

After logging in, you'll be prompted to activate your subscription:

- $50/month base subscription
- Metered API usage billed at cost (typically $5--15/month depending on usage)
- Billing is handled securely through Stripe

Click "Subscribe" to complete checkout. You'll be redirected back to Pantheon to begin onboarding.

Step 3: Complete the onboarding wizard

The onboarding wizard has three steps:

Team

- Team name -- A display name for your workspace

Agent

Discord

- Add Pantheon to Discord -- Click the invite link to add the Pantheon bot to your server, or paste your Discord Server ID directly. See the Discord Setup guide for details.

Step 4: Send your first message

Once onboarding is complete, your agent is ready. Open your Discord server and try one of these:

Your AI agent will respond in the channel. That's it -- you're up and running.

What's next?

- Custom Skills -- Create tailored skills for your agents
