title: Connecting Services

headings.title: Dashboard did not update after completing OAuth
Revoking access from the service side

body:
Connecting Services

Each toolkit that requires authentication needs a one-time OAuth connection before your agents can use it. This page covers the full connection lifecycle -- authorizing, monitoring, refreshing, and disconnecting services.

The OAuth flow

OAuth is the industry-standard protocol for granting third-party access to your accounts without sharing passwords. When you connect a service through Pantheon, you go through the service provider's own login and consent screens. Pantheon never sees or stores your service passwords.

Step by step

1. Initiate: In the Connected Accounts section, click Connect next to the service.
2. Confirm: A dialog shows what actions your agents will have access to. Click Authorize [Service].
3. Authenticate: A popup window opens with the service's login page (e.g., Google's account chooser).
4. Consent: Review the permissions requested. Google, for example, shows exactly which scopes are being requested (read spreadsheets, send email, etc.).
5. Grant access: Click Allow or Grant Access on the service's consent page.
6. Complete: The popup shows "Connected" and closes automatically. The parent page updates with a green "Connected" badge.

What happens behind the scenes

When you click Authorize:

1. Pantheon's API calls Composio to initiate an OAuth connection for your Composio entity.
2. Composio generates a redirect URL pointing to the service's OAuth authorization endpoint.
3. The popup navigates to this URL.
4. After you consent, the service redirects back to Pantheon's callback URL.
5. The callback endpoint syncs your connection status from Composio and updates the database.
6. The popup sends a postMessage to the parent window, which triggers a UI refresh.

OAuth access tokens and refresh tokens are stored and managed by Composio, not Pantheon. Composio handles token refresh automatically when tokens expire. You do not need to re-authorize unless the refresh token itself expires (which is rare for most services).

Which account to use

When the OAuth popup opens, you choose which account to connect. Some considerations:

Personal vs. shared accounts

| Scenario | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| Solo user | Use your personal Google/service account |
| Small team | Use a shared account (e.g., team@gmail.com) that all members can access |
| Organization with employees | Use a dedicated team account to avoid mixing personal and business data |

Multiple accounts

If you have multiple Google accounts (personal and work), make sure to select the correct one during the OAuth flow. The account chooser screen lets you pick which account to authorize.

Each toolkit connects to one account. If you connect Google Sheets with your personal account and later want to switch to a team account, disconnect the service first and then reconnect with the other account.

Managing connections

Viewing connection status

The Connected Accounts section in Settings > Integrations shows all selected toolkits that require authentication. Each entry displays:

- Service name -- The toolkit name (e.g., Google Sheets)
- Account identifier -- The email or username associated with the connection
- Status badge -- Current connection state
- Action button -- Connect or Disconnect

Refreshing status

Click the Refresh button at the top of the Connected Accounts section to sync the latest connection status from Composio. This is useful if:

- You completed an OAuth flow but the status has not updated
- You want to verify a connection is still active
- You disconnected from the service provider's side (e.g., revoked access in Google settings)

Disconnecting a service

To disconnect a service:

1. Find the service in the Connected Accounts list.
2. Click the Disconnect button.
3. The connection is revoked immediately.

Disconnecting does the following:

- Removes the OAuth tokens from Composio
- Updates your connection status to "Disconnected"
- Your agents can no longer access that service

Disconnecting does not deselect the toolkit. The toolkit remains selected in your configuration -- it just has no active connection. You can reconnect at any time.

When you disconnect a service, access is revoked immediately. There is no delay or grace period. Your agents will get an authorization error if they try to call that service's actions after disconnection.

Connection states

| State | Badge | Meaning | Action needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connected | Green | Active, working connection | None |
| Disconnected | Gray | No connection established | Click Connect |
| Expired | Amber | Token expired, needs re-auth | Click Connect to re-authorize |
| Pending | Blue | OAuth flow in progress | Complete the flow in the popup |

Expired connections

Most OAuth providers issue refresh tokens that Composio uses to automatically renew access. However, some scenarios cause expiration:

- You changed your service account password
- You revoked Pantheon's access in the service's security settings
- The refresh token exceeded its maximum lifetime (uncommon)
- The service provider rotated their OAuth credentials (rare)

To fix an expired connection, click Connect again. The OAuth flow is the same as the initial connection.

Popup troubleshooting

Popup blocked

Most browsers block popups by default. If the OAuth popup does not appear:

1. Check for a popup notification in your browser's address bar (usually a small icon).
2. Click it and select "Always allow popups from this site."
3. Try clicking Connect again.

If popups remain blocked, Pantheon falls back to a full-page redirect. You will be redirected away from the dashboard to complete the OAuth flow, then redirected back afterward.

Popup opened but stuck

If the popup opens but does not show the service's login page:

- Check your internet connection
- Try closing the popup and clicking Connect again
- Clear your browser cache for the service's domain (e.g., accounts.google.com)

Popup closed before completing

If you accidentally close the popup before granting consent:

- No connection is created
- No tokens are stored
- Click Connect again to restart the flow

Dashboard did not update after completing OAuth

If the popup showed "Connected" but the dashboard still shows "Disconnected":

1. Click the Refresh button in the Connected Accounts section
2. If that does not help, reload the entire page

Revoking access from the service side

You can also revoke Pantheon's access directly from the service provider:

| Service | Where to revoke |
|---|---|
| Google (Sheets, Calendar, Gmail, Drive) | Google Account > Security > Third-party apps |
| Slack | Slack workspace settings > Manage apps |
| Notion | Notion settings > My connections |
| Todoist | Todoist settings > Integrations |
| Airtable | Airtable account settings > API |

After revoking from the service side, click Refresh in Pantheon to update your connection status. The status will change to "Expired" or "Disconnected."

What's next

- Available Toolkits -- Full list of services and their actions
- Security and Privacy -- How credentials are stored and protected
- Setup Guide -- Complete setup walkthrough

title: Integrations

headings.title: What can your agents do with integrations?

body:
Integrations

Integrations let your AI agents connect to external services like Google Sheets, Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, and hundreds more. Instead of being limited to skills and local tools, your agents can read spreadsheets, send emails, create calendar events, and interact with the services you already use.

Pantheon integrations are powered by Composio, a platform that provides secure, authenticated access to 800+ third-party APIs through a single connection. Composio runs as a remote MCP server alongside your built-in tools.

How it works

When you enable integrations, Pantheon:

1. Creates a Composio entity for your account (no separate Composio sign-up required).
2. Provisions a remote MCP server that your agents can call.
3. Adds the server to your workspace configuration alongside your existing MCP servers.

From that point, your agents have access to the actions exposed by your selected toolkits. You authorize each service individually through a standard OAuth flow -- the same "Sign in with Google" experience you use elsewhere.

Key concepts

Toolkits

A toolkit is a bundle of related actions for a single service. For example, the Google Sheets toolkit includes actions for reading spreadsheets, writing data, and creating new sheets. You select which toolkits your agents have access to -- only selected toolkits are available.

Pantheon curates a set of toolkits with descriptions tailored to common use cases. See Available Toolkits for the full list.

Connections

A connection is an authorized link between your agents and a specific service. When you connect Google Sheets, for example, you go through Google's OAuth consent screen and grant your agents permission to access your spreadsheets. The connection is managed by Composio -- your Google credentials are never stored on Pantheon servers.

See Connecting Services for the full OAuth walkthrough.

Remote MCP servers

Composio runs as a remote MCP server over Server-Sent Events (SSE). Your agents connect to Composio's hosted endpoint, which routes requests to the appropriate service API.

Composio runs entirely on Composio's infrastructure.

Composio integration is included in your Pantheon subscription. The Composio API key is platform-level -- you do not need your own Composio account or API key.

What can your agents do with integrations?

Here are some examples of what teams can ask once integrations are connected:

| Service | Example prompt | What happens |
|---|---|---|
| Google Sheets | "Add today's metrics to the tracking spreadsheet" | Reads your data, writes rows to your Google Sheet |
| Google Sheets | "Pull the budget numbers from the planning sheet" | Reads specific cells/ranges from a spreadsheet |
| Gmail | "Email the project summary to the client" | Composes and sends an email with relevant data |
| Google Calendar | "Schedule the design review for next Tuesday at 2 PM" | Creates a calendar event with details |
| Google Calendar | "What's on the team calendar this week?" | Lists upcoming events |
| Slack | "Post today's summary to the updates channel" | Sends a message to a Slack channel |
| Notion | "Save this meeting notes summary to Notion" | Creates a page in your Notion workspace |

Integrations complement your existing skills. Your agents can combine data from custom skills with integration actions (write to spreadsheet, send email, create calendar event) in a single response.

Getting started

1. Go to Settings > Integrations in your Pantheon dashboard.
2. Toggle the Composio switch to enable the integration.
3. Select the toolkits you want your agents to access.
4. Click Save Toolkits to apply changes.
5. Connect each service by clicking Connect and completing the OAuth flow.

For detailed setup instructions, see Setup Guide.

Privacy and security

- Your service credentials (Google tokens, Slack tokens, etc.) are encrypted with AES-256-GCM and managed by Composio. They are never stored on Pantheon servers.
- Composio acts as a secure proxy -- your agents access services through authorized API calls only.
- Disconnecting a service immediately revokes all access and removes stored tokens.
- You control exactly which toolkits your agents can use. Disabling a toolkit removes the agents' ability to call those actions.

See Security and Privacy for full details.

What's next

- Setup Guide -- Step-by-step walkthrough of enabling Composio and connecting services
- Available Toolkits -- Full list of curated toolkits with use-case descriptions
- Connecting Services -- OAuth flow, popup handling, and troubleshooting
- Security and Privacy -- How credentials are stored, what data flows where, and how to revoke access

title: Security and Privacy

headings.title: When your assistant uses an integration
Can Pantheon employees access my Google Sheets / emails / calendar?
What happens to my data if I cancel my Pantheon subscription?
Can I use integrations without giving Pantheon access to my email?

body:
Security and Privacy

This page explains how Pantheon's Composio integration handles your credentials, what data flows between systems, and what controls you have over access.

Architecture overview

Three systems are involved in the integrations flow:

| System | What it stores | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| Pantheon | Composio entity ID, MCP server URL, toolkit selections, cached connection status | Manages configuration, serves the dashboard UI |
| Composio | OAuth tokens (access + refresh), connection metadata | Handles OAuth flows, stores credentials, proxies API calls, refreshes tokens |
| Service (Google, etc.) | Your account data | Processes API requests from Composio on your behalf |

What Pantheon does NOT store

Pantheon never stores:

- Your service passwords
- OAuth access tokens
- OAuth refresh tokens
- Service API keys
- Any credentials for Google, Slack, Notion, or other connected services

The only credential-related data Pantheon stores is the connection status (connected/disconnected/expired) and the account identifier (e.g., the email address associated with the connection). These are cached from Composio for display in the dashboard.

Credential encryption

Composio encrypts all stored credentials using AES-256-GCM encryption at rest. This is the same encryption standard used by banks and government systems. Credentials are decrypted only at the moment they are needed to make an API call on your behalf.

AES-256-GCM is an authenticated encryption algorithm that provides both confidentiality and integrity. It uses a 256-bit key, making brute-force attacks computationally infeasible. The GCM mode adds authentication, ensuring encrypted data cannot be tampered with.

Data flow

When your assistant uses an integration

Here is the complete data flow when your assistant performs an integration action (e.g., writing to a Google Sheet):

1. Your assistant decides it needs to call a Google Sheets action based on your Discord message.
2. The assistant sends a tool call request to the Composio MCP server via SSE (Server-Sent Events).
3. Composio receives the request, identifies your entity, and retrieves your stored OAuth token.
4. Composio makes the API call to Google Sheets using your token.
5. Google Sheets processes the request and returns the result.
6. Composio forwards the result back to your assistant via SSE.
7. Your assistant uses the result to compose its response in Discord.

What data Composio can see

Composio acts as a proxy. When processing your requests, Composio can see:

- The action being called (e.g., "read spreadsheet")
- The parameters of the action (e.g., spreadsheet ID, cell range)
- The response from the service (e.g., cell values)

Composio does not:

- Store the content of your requests or responses beyond what is needed for the immediate API call
- Share your data with other Composio users
- Use your data for training or analytics
- Access your connected services outside of explicit tool calls from your assistant

What Pantheon can see

Pantheon can see:

- That you have Composio enabled
- Which toolkits you have selected
- Which services are connected (status only, not tokens)
- Standard API usage metrics (request counts for billing)

Pantheon cannot see:

- The content of integration requests or responses (these flow directly between your assistant and Composio)
- Your OAuth tokens or service credentials
- Your service account data (spreadsheet contents, emails, calendar events, etc.)

Access controls

You control what is enabled

You have several layers of control over what your assistant can access through integrations:

| Control | What it does | How to change |
|---|---|---|
| Composio toggle | Enables/disables all integrations | Settings > Integrations > toggle switch |
| Toolkit selection | Controls which services are available | Settings > Integrations > Toolkits > Save |
| Service connection | Authorizes/revokes access to specific services | Settings > Integrations > Connect/Disconnect |
| OAuth consent | Grants specific permission scopes | During the OAuth flow (Google consent screen, etc.) |

Principle of least privilege

Each layer is independent:

- Disabling Composio removes all integration access, regardless of toolkit or connection state.
- Deselecting a toolkit removes that service's actions even if the connection is active.
- Disconnecting a service revokes access even if the toolkit is selected.
- You can limit OAuth scopes during the consent step (though this may prevent some actions from working).

Visit the Connected Accounts section periodically to review which services are connected and disconnect any you no longer need. Less access surface means less risk.

Disconnection and revocation

Disconnecting from Pantheon

When you click Disconnect in the Pantheon dashboard:

1. Pantheon calls Composio's API to revoke the connection.
2. Composio deletes the stored OAuth tokens.
3. The connection status updates to "Disconnected" in Pantheon.
4. Your assistant immediately loses access to that service.

Revoking from the service side

You can also revoke access directly from the service provider (e.g., Google Account > Security > Third-party apps). When you do:

1. The service invalidates the OAuth tokens.
2. Composio's next attempt to use the token will fail.
3. The connection status will show as "Expired" after a refresh.
4. Your assistant will receive an authorization error when trying to use the service.

Both methods are equally effective. Disconnecting from Pantheon is the recommended approach because it provides immediate UI feedback.

Transport security

Pantheon to Composio

All communication between Pantheon and Composio occurs over HTTPS (TLS 1.2+). The MCP server connection uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) over HTTPS.

Composio to services

Composio communicates with service APIs over HTTPS. Composio does not support unencrypted connections to service providers.

Your browser to Pantheon

The Pantheon dashboard is served over HTTPS. OAuth callbacks are also HTTPS-only.

Incident response

If Composio is compromised

In the unlikely event of a Composio security incident:

1. Immediately disconnect all services from the Pantheon dashboard.
2. Revoke Pantheon's access from each service provider's security settings.
3. Disable the Composio integration toggle.
4. Change passwords on your connected service accounts.

If Pantheon is compromised

A Pantheon compromise would not expose your service credentials because Pantheon does not store them. However, an attacker with access to your Pantheon account could:

- See which services are connected (status only)
- Enable/disable toolkits
- Trigger new OAuth flows (but would need your service password to complete them)

To mitigate: use a strong password and magic link authentication for your Pantheon account.

Frequently asked questions

Can Pantheon employees access my Google Sheets / emails / calendar?

No. Pantheon does not store your service credentials and does not have access to your connected service accounts. Only your AI assistant can make API calls through Composio using your authorized tokens.

What happens to my data if I cancel my Pantheon subscription?

When your subscription ends and your workspace is closed:

- Your Composio entity and all stored tokens are deleted.
- All service connections are revoked.
- No residual access to your services remains.

Can I use integrations without giving Pantheon access to my email?

Yes. Each toolkit is independent. You can enable Google Sheets and Google Calendar without enabling Gmail. You choose exactly which services to connect.

Is Composio SOC 2 / GDPR compliant?

Composio maintains enterprise security practices. Check composio.dev/security for their latest compliance certifications and security documentation.

What's next

- Integrations Overview -- How integrations fit into your Pantheon workspace
- Connecting Services -- OAuth flow and connection management
- Troubleshooting -- Common issues and solutions

title: Setup Guide

headings.title: Agent says it cannot access a service

body:
Setup Guide

This guide walks you through enabling Composio integrations, selecting toolkits, and connecting your first third-party service.

Prerequisites

Before you can enable integrations, you need:

- An active Pantheon subscription
- An active workspace (your AI agents must already be set up)
- Access to the Pantheon dashboard

If your workspace is not yet set up, complete the onboarding wizard first.

Step 1: Enable Composio

1. Navigate to Settings > Integrations in your Pantheon dashboard.
2. You will see the Composio integration card at the top of the page.
3. Toggle the switch to enable the integration.

When you enable Composio, Pantheon performs several setup steps behind the scenes:

- Creates a Composio entity for your account
- Provisions a remote MCP server endpoint
- Stores the server configuration in your database
- Adds the Composio MCP server to your workspace configuration

Enabling Composio takes just a few seconds. Your agents gain access to integration tools immediately.

This is a one-time setup. Once enabled, adding or removing toolkits and connecting services is instant.

Step 2: Select toolkits

After enabling Composio, the Toolkits section appears below the integration card.

1. Browse the available toolkits using the category tabs: Recommended, Productivity, Communication, Data, or All.
2. Click on a toolkit card to select or deselect it.
3. Selected toolkits show a green checkmark and highlighted border.
4. Click Save Toolkits when you have made your selections.

Only the actions from your selected toolkits will be available to your agents.

The Recommended tab shows the toolkits most useful for common workflows: Google Sheets, Google Calendar, Gmail, and Slack. These cover the most common integration use cases -- spreadsheet tracking, scheduling, email, and notifications.

Changing toolkit selections

You can add or remove toolkits at any time:

1. Select or deselect toolkits as needed.
2. The Save Toolkits button becomes active when your selections differ from the saved configuration.
3. Click Save Toolkits to apply changes.

Removing a toolkit does not disconnect the service. If you have Google Sheets connected and you deselect the Google Sheets toolkit, the connection remains intact but the agents lose the ability to call Google Sheets actions. Re-selecting the toolkit restores access without needing to reconnect.

Step 3: Connect a service

After selecting toolkits, the Connected Accounts section shows all selected toolkits that require authentication.

1. Find the service you want to connect (e.g., Google Sheets).
2. Click the Connect button next to it.
3. A confirmation dialog appears showing:
- The service name
- The specific actions your agents will have access to
- A privacy note about how credentials are handled
4. Click Authorize [Service Name] to proceed.

OAuth popup flow

After clicking Authorize:

1. A popup window opens with the service's login/consent page (e.g., Google's "Choose an account" screen).
2. Sign in to your account if prompted.
3. Review the permissions requested and click Allow or Grant Access.
4. The popup displays a "Connected" message and closes automatically after 2 seconds.
5. The parent page updates to show the service as connected with a green "Connected" badge.
6. A success toast notification appears: "Service connected successfully".

The OAuth flow uses a popup window. If your browser blocks the popup, Pantheon falls back to a full-page redirect. To avoid interruption, allow popups for your Pantheon dashboard URL.

Connection status indicators

After connecting, each service shows a status badge:

| Badge | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Connected (green) | Active connection. Your agents can use this service. |
| Expired (amber) | Token has expired. Click Connect to re-authorize. |
| Disconnected (gray) | Not connected. Click Connect to start the OAuth flow. |
| Pending (blue) | OAuth flow in progress. |

Step 4: Test the integration

Once a service is connected, test it by sending a message to your agent in Discord:

Google Sheets test

Gmail test

Google Calendar test

If the agent responds with data from the service, your integration is working correctly.

Disabling Composio

To disable the entire Composio integration:

1. Navigate to Settings > Integrations.
2. Toggle the Composio switch to off.
3. The Composio MCP server is removed from your workspace configuration.
4. Your agents lose access to all integration actions.

Disabling Composio does not disconnect your service accounts. If you re-enable Composio later, your existing connections will still be active. To fully disconnect, remove each service connection before disabling.

Troubleshooting

"Failed to enable integration"

This usually means the Composio API is temporarily unavailable. Wait a minute and try again. If the issue persists, check the Troubleshooting page.

OAuth popup does not appear

Your browser may be blocking popups. Check your browser's popup blocker settings and allow popups for your Pantheon dashboard domain.

Service shows "Expired"

OAuth tokens expire periodically. Click Connect next to the expired service to re-authorize. The flow is the same as the initial connection.

Agent says it cannot access a service

Verify that:

1. Composio is enabled (toggle is on).
2. The toolkit for that service is selected and saved.
3. The service is connected (green "Connected" badge).
4. You have saved your toolkit selections since the last change.

If all four conditions are met and the agent still cannot access the service, click Refresh in the Connected Accounts section to sync the latest connection status from Composio.

What's next

- Available Toolkits -- Full list of services with use-case descriptions
- Connecting Services -- Deep dive into the OAuth flow and connection management
- Security and Privacy -- How credentials are protected

title: Available Toolkits
body:
Available Toolkits

Pantheon curates a set of Composio toolkits with descriptions and recommendations for common team workflows. Each toolkit bundles the most useful actions for a single service.

Recommended toolkits

These four toolkits cover the most common integration use cases. They are highlighted in the Recommended tab when selecting toolkits.

Google Sheets

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Recommended |
| Requires auth | Yes (Google account) |
| Actions | Read Spreadsheet, Write Spreadsheet, Create Spreadsheet |

What it does: Read and write Google Sheets spreadsheets directly from Discord.

Use cases:

- Track project deliverables and metrics in a shared spreadsheet
- Log daily observations and activity data
- Pull budget numbers from a planning spreadsheet
- Build summary reports that update automatically

Example prompts:

Google Sheets works great alongside custom skills. Ask your agent to "export this week's data to my tracking spreadsheet" and it will read your skill data and write to Google Sheets in one step.

Google Calendar

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Recommended |
| Requires auth | Yes (Google account) |
| Actions | Create Event, List Events, Update Event |

What it does: Manage Google Calendar events -- create, read, and update scheduling entries.

Use cases:

Example prompts:

|---|---|

What it does: Send and read Gmail messages through your agents.

Use cases:

- Forward project summaries to stakeholders
- Email daily or weekly reports to team members
- Share updates with external collaborators
- Send notifications about important events

Example prompts:

When your agent sends an email through Gmail, it sends from your actual Gmail account. Double-check recipients and content before confirming sends, especially for the first few uses.

Slack

|---|---|
| Category | Communication |

What it does: Post messages to Slack channels from your agents.

Example prompts:

Productivity toolkits

Notion

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Productivity |

| Actions | Create Page, Search, Update Page |

What it does: Create and search Notion pages from your agents.

Use cases:

- Build a searchable knowledge base of notes and SOPs
- Document plans and strategies

- Keep running notes on project progress

Example prompts:

Google Drive

| | |
|---|---|
| Category | Productivity |
| Requires auth | Yes (Google account) |

What it does: Access and organize files in Google Drive.

Data toolkits

Airtable

What it does: Read and write records in Airtable bases.

Use cases:

- Manage inventory with structured records
- Track project data across teams and timelines

- Build custom databases for any tracking need

Example prompts:

Toolkit selection tips

Start small

Enable only the toolkits you will actively use. Each toolkit adds actions to your agent's tool list, and a smaller tool list means faster, more focused responses. You can always add more toolkits later.

Recommended first

The four recommended toolkits (Google Sheets, Google Calendar, Gmail, Slack) cover 90% of integration use cases for most teams. Start with these and expand as needed.

Combine with skills

The most powerful workflows combine Pantheon skills with integration actions:

| Skill | + Integration | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Custom data skill | Google Sheets | Auto-export data to spreadsheets |
| Scheduling skill | Google Calendar | Schedule tasks around availability |
| Reporting skill | Gmail | Email reports to stakeholders |
| Briefing skill | Slack | Cross-post updates to team channels |

What's next

- Setup Guide -- Enable Composio and select your toolkits
- Connecting Services -- Authorize your accounts

title: Filesystem Server
body:
Filesystem Server

The Filesystem MCP server gives your AI agent the ability to read, write, and manage files. This is useful for saving reports, keeping notes, storing exported data, and working with files that persist across conversations.

Overview

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Package | @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem |
| Command | npx |
| Arguments | -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem |
| Scope | Workspace (available to all agents) |

The Filesystem server is a custom MCP server you can add to your workspace if you need file read/write capabilities. It is not required for any built-in platform tools -- memory, knowledge files, and email all work without it.

What it enables

With the Filesystem server active, your agent can:

- Read files -- Open and read the contents of any file in its workspace directory.
- Write files -- Create new files or overwrite existing ones. Useful for saving reports, exporting data, or generating documents.
- List directory contents -- Browse what files and folders exist in the workspace.
- Create directories -- Organize files into folders as needed.
- Move and rename files -- Restructure workspace content without manual intervention.

Use cases

Saving reports

Ask your agent to generate a summary and save it to a file:

> "Summarize this week's key metrics and save the report to reports/weekly-summary-2025-10-13.md."

The agent will compile the data and write it to the specified path.

Exporting data

If you want a local copy of records or reports:

> "Export all completed tasks from October as a CSV file."

The agent writes the CSV to the workspace where it persists between conversations.

Keeping operational notes

Your agent can maintain running notes, checklists, or logs:

> "Add a note to meeting-notes.md that the design review is rescheduled to Thursday."

Files saved by the Filesystem server persist between conversations and are available any time you interact with your agent.

Adding the Filesystem server

1. Go to Settings > MCP Servers.
2. Click Add MCP Server.
3. Select From Preset.
4. Click Filesystem from the preset list.
5. Review the pre-filled fields and click Add Server.

The server is available to your agent after saving.

Enabling and disabling

You can toggle the Filesystem server on or off at any time using the switch on its card in Settings > MCP Servers. Disabling the server removes it from your agent's active tools. Your files are not deleted when the server is disabled.

What's next

- MCP Servers Overview -- Learn about custom MCP server configuration

title: MCP Servers
body:
MCP Servers

Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers let you extend your AI agents with custom tools and data access beyond the built-in platform tools. MCP servers are for advanced or custom tooling -- the core capabilities (memory, knowledge files, email, and custom skills) are handled natively by the Pantheon tenant runtime and do not require any MCP configuration.

What is MCP?

MCP is an open protocol that lets AI agents connect to external tool servers using a standardized interface. Instead of building every tool directly into the AI model, MCP servers expose capabilities that the agent can call on demand. This makes it possible to integrate specialized or third-party tools without modifying the core agent.

When your agent needs to use a custom tool provided by an MCP server, it sends a request to that server. The server processes the request and returns the result, which the agent then uses to answer your question.

Memory, knowledge files, email, and custom skills are all built into the Pantheon platform. They work out of the box with no MCP server setup. MCP servers are only needed if you want to add custom or third-party tools.

When to use MCP servers

MCP servers are useful when you need your agents to access tools or data sources that are not covered by the built-in platform tools. Examples include:

- A custom internal API for your business software
- A specialized data source or database
- Third-party tool servers published by the MCP community
- Filesystem access for saving and reading custom files

Adding a custom MCP server

MCP servers are managed through the Pantheon dashboard.

1. Go to Settings > MCP Servers.
2. Click Add MCP Server.
3. Fill in the required fields and click Add Server.

You can enable, disable, edit, or delete servers at any time.

When adding a custom server, you will need to provide:

| Field | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| serverkey | Unique lowercase identifier (letters, numbers, hyphens) | my-custom-server |
| displayname | Human-readable label shown in the dashboard | My Custom Server |
| command | The executable to run | npx |
| args | Arguments passed to the command (space-separated) | -y @my-org/my-mcp-server /data |
| envvars | Optional environment variables for the server process | {"APIKEY": "..."} |
| scope | instance (all agents) or agent (specific agent only) | instance |
| enabled | Whether the server is active | true |

The serverkey cannot be changed after creation. If you need a different key, delete the server and create a new one.

Scoping

MCP servers are defined at the workspace level by default, meaning every agent in a multi-agent setup can use them. You can also scope a server to a specific agent if you want to restrict access.

Each server is identified by a unique serverkey. The key is used internally to reference the server in the agent's configuration.

What's next

- Filesystem Server -- Example: read and write files in a workspace directory
- Extensions -- Add more capabilities through the extension marketplace

title: Custom Skills
body:
Custom Skills

Pantheon lets you create custom skills using the Skill Forge. Custom skills extend your agents with new capabilities tailored to your specific workflows.

What are custom skills?

A custom skill is a set of instructions written in Markdown (SKILL.md format) that tells your agent how to handle a specific type of request. You can create up to 20 active custom skills per workspace, each up to 50KB in size.

Creating a skill

Using AI generation

The fastest way to create a custom skill is with AI generation:

1. Go to Settings > Skills > Forge
2. Click Create Skill
3. Select Generate with AI
4. Describe what you want the skill to do in plain English (10--5,000 characters)
5. Optionally toggle Include team context to auto-include your team name and description
6. Pantheon streams a complete SKILL.md in real time
7. Review the generated skill, make any adjustments, and save

The more detail you provide, the better the generated skill. Include examples of questions the skill should handle, what data it should use, and how responses should be formatted.

Writing manually

You can also write a skill from scratch using the built-in Markdown editor with formatting toolbar (bold, italic, headings, code blocks, lists) and live preview.

Using templates

Pantheon provides built-in templates for common skill patterns:

| Template | Category |
|---|---|
| Report Generator | Reporting |
| Data Tracker | Data Management |
| Research Assistant | Research |
| Task Manager | Productivity |
| Meeting Notes | Productivity |
| Client Brief | Communication |
| Compliance Checker | Compliance |
| Inventory Tracker | Operations |

Select a template when creating a new skill to get a working starting point you can customize.

SKILL.md format

Custom skills use a Markdown-based format with YAML frontmatter:

The name field must start with custom- and match the skill's slug.

Version history

Every time you save changes to a custom skill, Pantheon creates a new version. From the version history panel you can:

- View all versions -- See each version with its timestamp and change summary
- Compare -- The current version is highlighted for easy identification
- Roll back -- Click the rollback button on any previous version to restore it. Rollbacks create a new version so you never lose history.

Testing

Test your skill before deploying it to your live agents:

1. Open the skill in the Forge editor
2. Switch to the Test tab
3. Enter a sample message that should trigger the skill
4. Watch the agent's streaming response in real time
5. Continue the conversation with follow-up messages to iterate

The test panel shows a full chat interface. Draft skills run in preview mode; active skills test against your live agents.

Skill lifecycle

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Draft | Created but not assigned to agents. Testing available. |
| Active | Deployed and available for agent assignment. |
| Archived | Soft-deleted. Cannot be updated. Automatically removed from all agents. |

Reference files

You can attach up to 10 reference files (max 50KB each) to a custom skill to give your agent additional context -- product manuals, lookup tables, conversion charts, or domain-specific documentation.

What's next

- Knowledge Files -- Upload documents for your agents to reference
- AI Agents -- Assign skills to specific agents

title: Skills
body:
Skills

Skills define what your AI agents can do. Each skill is a Markdown-based instruction set (SKILL.md format) that teaches an agent how to perform a specific task — from looking up FAQs to generating reports to processing inbound requests.

All skills are custom

Pantheon's skill system is 100% custom. You create skills tailored to your exact use case. There are no locked-in built-in skills.

Three ways to create skills

| Method | How it works |
|--------|-------------|
| Skill Forge | Write a skill from scratch using the built-in Markdown editor |
| AI Generation | Describe what you want in plain English and Pantheon generates the skill |
| Extension Marketplace | Install pre-built skills shared by the community |

Custom skills are included in the $50/month subscription. The only variable cost is metered API usage when agents use skills to process messages.

How skills work

Each skill contains:

- Instructions — Detailed guidance that tells the agent how to perform the task, what data to look for, and how to format output
- Tools — Access to APIs, databases, or file creation capabilities needed to complete the task
- Configuration — Any settings or parameters the skill needs

When you ask an agent a question, it determines which of its assigned skills applies and follows the skill's instructions to produce a response.

Managing skills

Creating a skill

1. Go to Settings > Skills
2. Click Create Skill or Generate with AI
3. Write or generate the skill content
4. Save

Assigning skills to agents

Skills are assigned per-agent. Each agent only has access to its assigned skills.

1. Go to Agents in the sidebar
2. Select the agent you want to configure
3. Under Skills, toggle each skill on or off
4. Save

This lets you create focused specialists:

- A Support Lead with "FAQ Lookup" and "Ticket Triage" skills
- A Research Analyst with "Web Search" and "Report Generator" skills
- A General Assistant with all skills for catch-all questions

Version history

Every skill edit creates a new version. You can view the version history and roll back to any previous version from the skill editor.

Skills and agents

If you run a multi-agent setup, each agent gets its own subset of skills:

| Configuration level | Where to change it | What it controls |
|---|---|---|
| Skill creation | Settings > Skills | Creates and manages the skill definition |
| Per-agent assignment | Agents > [Agent] > Skills | Which agents can use the skill |

A skill created in Settings > Skills is available for assignment to any agent. Deleting a skill removes it from all agents.

What's next

- Custom Skills — Deep dive into creating and managing skills
- Skills & Scheduling — Assign skills to specific agents
- Extension Marketplace — Browse and install community skills

title: Email Integration
body:
Email Integration

Pantheon's email integration lets your AI agent send and receive email. Set up an email inbox, and your agent can process inbound emails with attachments and compose replies -- all accessible from Discord.

Setting up your email inbox

Email is optional and only enabled by explicit action:

1. Go to Settings > Email
2. Click Enable Email Inbox
3. Pantheon generates a unique email address (e.g., acme-team@pantheon.app)
4. Your inbox is ready

The default slug is based on your team name. You can customize it to any available slug (3--63 characters, lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens).

How inbound email works

When someone sends an email to your Pantheon address:

1. The email is received and processed (including attachments)
2. Your agent sends an immediate acknowledgment reply ("Got your file. Analyzing now...")
3. The content and attachments are analyzed by your agent
4. A full response is sent back via email with proper threading

Common attachments like PDFs, images, Word documents, and text files are automatically read and summarized.

Sending email from Discord

Ask your agent to send emails directly:

Your agent composes the email based on your request and sends it from your Pantheon email identity.

Customizing your email address

To change your email slug:

1. Go to Settings > Email
2. Edit the slug field
3. Save

The slug must be unique across all Pantheon customers. Changes take effect immediately.

The email integration is included in your $50/month subscription at no additional cost. Only the AI processing of email content counts toward metered usage.

Privacy and security

- Email processing happens within your workspace -- no data is shared between customers
- Inbound emails and attachments are stored with Row-Level Security
- Webhook signatures are verified for all inbound email events
- Idempotency keys prevent duplicate processing

What's next

- Integrations -- Connect to Google Sheets, Calendar, and more
- Knowledge Files -- Upload reference documents

title: Data Exports
body:
Data Exports

Pantheon lets you export your workspace data for backup, analysis, or migration. Export conversations, knowledge files, memory records, and metadata as JSONL or CSV files.

Creating an export

1. Go to Settings > Exports
2. Choose the scope:
- Full tenant -- All data including conversations, knowledge, memory, and metadata
- Knowledge + memory only -- Just your knowledge files and memory vault records
- Metadata only -- Configuration and account data without conversation content
3. Choose the format: JSONL or CSV
4. Optionally check Include blobs to include binary attachments and files
5. Click Queue Export

Exports are processed in the background. You can navigate away and check back later.

Export lifecycle

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Queued | Waiting to start |
| Running | Currently being processed |
| Completed | Ready to download |
| Failed | Job errored -- you can retry |
| Expired | Old exports are automatically cleaned up after the retention period |

Downloading exports

Once an export shows "Completed":

1. Click on the export to view details
2. Download the manifest file and individual data files
3. Each file includes a checksum for integrity verification

What's next

- Knowledge Files -- Manage your uploaded documents
- Memory Vault -- View and manage persistent memories
- Billing -- Manage your subscription

title: Tools
body:
Tools

Pantheon tools are delivered through skills. Each skill gives your agent a focused capability and the workflow needed to execute it reliably.

Tool inventory

Pantheon provides a set of core platform tools that every workspace can use:

| Tool | What it does | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| Custom Skills | Create your own skills with AI-assisted generation | Tailoring your agents to your specific workflows |
| Knowledge Files | Upload documents your agents can reference | Providing context from existing docs, manuals, and reference material |
| Memory Vault | Persistent facts and context across conversations | Remembering preferences, decisions, and ongoing context |
| Email | Send and receive email through your agents | Communicating with people outside Discord |
| Secrets Vault | Store API keys for authenticated external API calls | Connecting to external services securely |
| Spending Cap | Set monthly usage limits | Controlling costs |

How tools are managed

Tool access follows the same two-step model as skills:

1. Global toggle in Settings > Skills controls whether the tool is enabled for the workspace.
2. Per-agent assignment in Settings > Agents controls which agents can use each enabled tool.

An explicit inventory of tools plus clear policy on how access is controlled.

Custom skills

Beyond the platform tools, you can create custom skills to extend your agents with domain-specific capabilities. Custom skills are written in Markdown (SKILL.md format) and can be generated with AI assistance. See Custom Skills for details.

Related docs

- Custom Skills -- Create tailored skills for your agents
- Skills & Scheduling -- Assign tools per agent and configure cron jobs
- MCP Servers -- Custom tool servers for advanced use cases
- Visual Workflow Builder -- Guided automation authoring walkthrough
- Workflow Builder Rollout Rings -- Canary/standard/delayed release controls
- Workflow KPI Review Cadence -- Weekly launch readiness review workflow

title: Knowledge Files
body:
Knowledge Files

Knowledge files let you upload documents that your agents can reference when answering questions. Upload manuals, guides, policies, reference material, or any other documents you want your agents to know about.

Supported formats

| Format | Extension | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| PDF | .pdf | Text extracted automatically (with OCR fallback for scanned documents) |
| Word Document | .docx | Full text extraction |
| Plain Text | .txt | Direct text |
| Markdown | .md | Supports frontmatter |

Limits

| Limit | Value |
|---|---|
| Max file size (upload) | 10 MB |
| Max parsed content per file | 500 KB |
| Max total storage | 2 MB across all files |
| Max number of files | 20 |

Uploading files

1. Go to Settings > Knowledge
2. Drag and drop a file or click to browse
3. Choose the scope: Shared (all agents) or a specific agent
4. Upload

Pantheon processes the document, extracts the text, sanitizes the content, and indexes it for retrieval. Your agents can reference the content immediately.

Shared vs. per-agent knowledge

| Scope | Who can access | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| Shared | All agents in your workspace | General documents, SOPs, reference material |
| Per-agent | Only the assigned agent | Specialized documents relevant to one agent's role |

You can reassign files between shared and per-agent at any time using the dropdown on each file's card.

If you delete an agent that has per-agent knowledge files, those files are automatically promoted to shared so they remain accessible.

How retrieval works

When your agent receives a question, it searches your knowledge files using a hybrid approach:

- Semantic search -- Finds content that is conceptually similar to the question using vector embeddings, even if different words are used
- Keyword search -- Matches specific terms and phrases from the question

The most relevant passages are included in the agent's context when generating a response. Your agent will cite the source document when using knowledge file content.

Large documents are automatically split into chunks for efficient retrieval. No configuration needed.

Managing knowledge files

From Settings > Knowledge, you can:

- View all uploaded files with their type, size, status, and agent assignment
- Monitor storage -- A progress bar shows your total usage
- Reassign scope -- Move files between shared and per-agent
- Delete files you no longer need
- Review failed uploads -- See error messages and retry or remove

A few well-chosen, relevant documents will give better results than uploading everything you have. Focus on documents your agents are likely to need when answering questions.

What's next

- Memory Vault -- Persistent facts and context across conversations
- Custom Skills -- Create new capabilities for your agents
- AI Agents -- Assign knowledge to specific agents

title: Memory Vault
body:
Memory Vault

The Memory Vault gives your agents persistent memory across conversations. Important facts, preferences, and context are stored and automatically recalled when relevant -- so your agents don't forget what you told them yesterday.

How it works

During conversations, your agents store observations in the Memory Vault. Each memory is classified by type and indexed with semantic embeddings for retrieval by meaning.

When your agent receives a new message, it searches the vault for relevant memories and includes them in its context. This means your agents can:

- Remember your preferences ("I prefer weekly reports on Mondays")
- Recall past decisions ("We decided to use vendor X for the Q2 project")
- Track ongoing situations ("The website migration is scheduled for next Friday")
- Build on previous conversations without re-explaining context

Memory types

| Type | Example |
|---|---|
| Fact | "Team is a 10-person marketing agency" |
| Preference | "Always format reports as bullet points" |
| Commitment | "Agreed to deliver the proposal by March 15" |
| Outcome | "Q4 campaign achieved 12% conversion rate" |
| Summary | "October project recap: 3 projects completed, 2 in progress" |

Memory tiers

Memories are automatically classified into tiers based on confidence and type:

| Tier | What goes here |
|---|---|
| Knowledge | High-confidence facts and strong preferences |
| Episodic | Conversations, events, and moderate-confidence observations |
| Working | Short-term context, low-confidence items |

Higher-tier memories are prioritized during retrieval.

Retrieval

Your agents automatically search the Memory Vault when processing messages. You can also ask directly:

The vault uses hybrid retrieval:
1. Semantic search finds conceptually similar memories using vector embeddings
2. Keyword search matches specific terms
3. Results are merged, scored, and the top matches are included in context

Settings

Configure the Memory Vault from Settings > Memory:

| Setting | Options | Default |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | Native only / Hybrid local vault | Native only |
| Capture level | Conservative / Standard / Aggressive | Standard |
| Retention days | 7--3,650 days | 365 |
| Exclude categories | Comma-separated list (e.g., "secrets, otp") | Empty |
| Auto-checkpoint | On / Off | On |
| Auto-compress | On / Off | On |

Compression and checkpoints

Over time, the vault accumulates many memories. The compression system:

- Groups related memories together
- Deduplicates semantically similar records
- Promotes episodic memories to knowledge tier when confidence increases
- Tombstones low-confidence working-tier records that are no longer useful
- Creates checkpoints so you can review the vault state at any point

Compression runs automatically when enabled, or you can trigger it manually from the settings page.

Diagnostics

The Memory settings page includes a diagnostics panel showing:

- Total records (active and tombstoned)
- Records added in the last 24 hours
- Average confidence score
- Tier distribution breakdown
- Latest record timestamp

Privacy

- Memories are stored per-workspace and never shared between customers
- All memory data is stored with Row-Level Security in your database
- Exact-duplicate detection prevents redundant storage
- You can delete any or all memories at any time

What's next

- Knowledge Files -- Upload reference documents
- Custom Skills -- Create new agent capabilities
- AI Agents -- Configure your agent team

title: Schedules & Briefings
body:
Schedules & Briefings

Schedules let you automate recurring messages from your agents. The main feature is the daily briefing -- a configurable summary posted to your Discord channel on a schedule you define.

Daily briefing

The briefing is a single scheduled message that combines multiple sections. Configure it from Settings > Schedules.

Briefing sections

Toggle each section on or off. The available sections depend on the custom skills you have enabled. Common examples include:

| Section | What it includes |
|---|---|
| Summary | A digest of recent activity, key updates, and pending items |
| Custom reports | Data pulled from your configured skills and knowledge files |

Configuration

| Setting | Options |
|---|---|
| Enable/disable | Toggle the entire briefing on or off |
| Send time | Time of day (e.g., 06:30) |
| Timezone | America/Chicago, America/Denver, America/New\York, America/Los\Angeles |
| Discord channel | The channel ID where the briefing is posted |

To get a Discord channel ID: enable Developer Mode in Discord (User Settings > Advanced), then right-click the channel and click "Copy Channel ID".

Per-agent cron jobs

In addition to the briefing system, individual agents can have their own scheduled jobs. You can configure any agent to run a scheduled task at a specific time and frequency. Common examples:

- Morning summary briefings
- Daily status reports
- Weekly digest messages
- Recurring data collection tasks

Configure per-agent cron jobs from Settings > Agents on each agent's card. Times are in your workspace's configured timezone.

Cost considerations

Each scheduled run consumes API tokens:

- A typical briefing: 1,500--3,000 tokens
- Data-heavy reports: 2,000--4,000 tokens
- Full briefing (all sections): 3,000--6,000 tokens

Factor this into your spending cap and usage estimates. Disabling unused schedules is the easiest way to reduce costs.

What's next

- Skills & Scheduling -- Per-agent schedule configuration
- Metered Usage -- Understand how schedules affect costs
- Spending Cap -- Set budget limits

title: Secrets Vault

headings.title: What agents can and cannot see

body:
Secrets Vault

The Secrets Vault lets you store API keys, tokens, and passwords that your agents can use when calling external APIs. Your agents never see the raw credential — they work with opaque handles, and the server injects the real value into HTTP requests behind the scenes.

How it works

Credential injection follows a three-step flow:

1. Store a secret — you add a label, the raw value, and an injection scheme (e.g. Bearer token) in Settings > Secrets Vault.
2. Agent gets a handle — when an agent needs to call an external API, it calls the usecredential tool with the secret's label. It receives an opaque handle ID — not the raw value.
3. Server injects the credential — the agent passes the handle to the httprequest tool. The server resolves the handle, decrypts the secret, injects it into the correct HTTP header, and sends the request. The raw value never enters the LLM context.

Adding a secret

Navigate to Settings > Secrets Vault and click Add Secret.

| Field | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Label | Yes | A short identifier (e.g. climate-fieldview, my-weather-api). Letters, numbers, hyphens, and underscores only. |
| Description | No | A human-readable note for your reference. |
| Secret Value | Yes | The raw API key, token, or password. Encrypted immediately — never stored in plaintext. |
| Injection Scheme | Yes | How the credential is attached to outgoing HTTP requests (see table below). |
| Allowed Domains | No | Restrict which hosts this credential can be sent to. |
| Allowed Agents | No | Restrict which agents can request a handle for this secret. |

Injection schemes

| Scheme | HTTP result | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Bearer Token | Authorization: Bearer sk-abc123 | Most REST APIs (OpenAI, Stripe, etc.) |
| Basic Auth | Authorization: Basic base64(user:pass) | APIs using HTTP Basic authentication |
| Custom Header | X-API-Key: sk-abc123 | APIs that use a named header for auth |
| Query Parameter | ?apikey=sk-abc123 | APIs that accept keys in the URL (less common) |

Domain scoping

You can restrict each secret to specific domains. When allowed domains are set, the httprequest tool will refuse to send the credential to any other host.

Always scope credentials to the domains that need them. For example, if your API key is for api.fieldview.com, set that as the allowed domain. This prevents the credential from being sent anywhere else, even if the agent is tricked by a prompt injection.

Agent scoping

If you run multiple agents (e.g. Support Bot, Research Agent), you can restrict which agents can request a handle for each secret. When allowed agents are set, only those agents can use the credential — others will receive an error.

Leave the agent list empty to allow all agents.

Security architecture

Secret values are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a server-side key. The encrypted ciphertext is never exposed through any API endpoint. Only the masked hint (e.g. sk-a23) is returned to the dashboard.

Key security properties:

- Ephemeral handles — credential handles expire after 60 seconds and can only be consumed once.
- SSRF protection — the httprequest tool blocks requests to private IP ranges and requires HTTPS.
- Response redaction — if a secret value appears in an API response, it is automatically stripped before the response reaches the LLM.
- Audit trail — every handle creation, credential injection, and reveal attempt is logged in tenantsecretauditlog with tenant, agent, and run context.

What agents can and cannot see

| Visible to agent | Not visible to agent |
|---|---|
| Secret label | Raw secret value |
| Injection scheme (e.g. "bearer") | Encrypted ciphertext |
| Handle ID (opaque reference) | Credentials echoed in HTTP responses |
| Allowed domains list | |

Break-glass access

Break-glass access puts the raw secret value directly into the LLM context. This should only be used when credential injection via httprequest is not possible — for example, when the agent needs to pass an API key inside a request body rather than a header.

The revealsecret tool is an escape hatch for cases where handle-based injection is not sufficient. It is:

- Disabled by default — you must explicitly enable it in Settings > Secrets Vault.
- Approval-gated — every call requires owner approval before execution.
- Fully audited — every reveal is logged with the agent, run, and justification reason.
- Redacted post-turn — after the LLM turn completes, revealed values are stripped from stored messages and conversation traces.
- Rate-limited — maximum 5 reveals per hour per tenant.

Only secrets with usagemode set to "Break glass" can be revealed. Standard injection-only secrets cannot be revealed even if the tool is enabled.

FAQ

Can my agent leak my API key?
No, under normal operation. The usecredential → httprequest flow keeps the raw value entirely server-side. The agent only sees a handle ID. Even if the agent's conversation is compromised, the attacker gets an opaque handle that expires in 60 seconds, not the actual credential. If you enable break-glass access, the raw value does enter the LLM context for that turn, but it is redacted from stored messages afterward.

What if I delete a secret mid-conversation?
Any outstanding handles for that secret will fail when the httprequest tool tries to consume them. The agent will receive an error message and can inform the user.

How many secrets can I store?
There is no hard limit on the number of secrets per workspace. Each secret adds negligible storage overhead.

Are secrets included in my subscription?
Yes. The Secrets Vault is included in all Pantheon plans at no additional cost. Only the API calls made through httprequest count toward your usage metering.

What's next

- Custom Skills — build skills that use your stored credentials
- Knowledge Files — upload reference documents for your agents
- Email — send and receive email through your assistant

title: Workflow KPI Review Cadence
body:
Workflow KPI Review Cadence

Run a weekly launch review every Monday at 16:00 UTC.

Required KPIs

- Time to first publish (median)
- Draft to publish completion rate
- Run success rate
- Retry rate
- Retry recovery success rate
- Approval cycle time (p50)
- Weekly active builders
- Estimated operator hours saved

Meeting agenda

1. Check KPI trend deltas week-over-week.
2. Review performance gates and sample sufficiency.
3. Decide rollout action: promote, hold, or rollback ring.
4. Assign follow-up owners and due dates.

Escalation thresholds

Escalate to incident response if:

- run success rate drops by 10+ points week-over-week
- retry recovery falls below 70%
- approval cycle p50 doubles week-over-week
- any gate remains failed across two reviews

title: Workflow Builder Rollout Rings
body:
Workflow Builder Rollout Rings

Pantheon uses three rollout rings to release workflow builder updates safely:

- Canary: first 10% of customers by deterministic customer-ID bucket.
- Standard: next 60% of customers.
- Delayed: remaining 30% of customers after gates pass.

Target ring control

The environment variable WORKFLOWBUILDERROLLOUTTARGETRING controls how far release is opened:

- canary: canary customers only.
- standard: canary + standard.
- delayed: all rings.

If unset or invalid, Pantheon defaults to delayed.

Promotion checklist

Before promoting to the next ring:

1. Check launch-readiness dashboard for KPI regressions.
2. Confirm performance gates (INP, LCP, CLS) are passing or have an approved exception.
3. Confirm no P1/P2 workflow incidents are open.
4. Confirm retry recovery and approval cycle metrics are stable week-over-week.

Rollback rule

If P1 incidents are detected or gate failures persist across two checks, set
WORKFLOWBUILDERROLLOUTTARGETRING back to the prior ring and hold promotion
until runbook actions are complete.

title: Visual Workflow Builder
body:
Visual Workflow Builder

Use Pantheon's visual workflow builder to design automations without calling APIs.

Guided walkthrough

1. Open Settings > Workflows (/settings/workflows).
2. Select New workflow and choose a starter template.
3. Add or edit nodes in the canvas, then resolve validation errors in the right panel.
4. Click Publish to promote the current draft and trigger runtime deploy.
5. Click Run or open Runs to verify execution and artifacts.
6. Open Launch readiness to review rollout ring and KPI baseline.

Keyboard map

- Cmd/Ctrl + S save draft
- Cmd/Ctrl + Z undo
- Shift + Cmd/Ctrl + Z redo
- Cmd/Ctrl + D duplicate selected node
- Cmd/Ctrl + K open command palette
- F fit canvas
- + / - zoom
- Esc clear selection / close palette

Recovery controls

- Retry from here: retry a failed/canceled run from a specific step.
- Rerun workflow: queue a fresh run for the latest published version.
- Rollback: move published version to the prior release and redeploy.

Related docs

- Workflow rollout rings
- Workflow builder support runbook

title: Common Issues

headings.title: I didn't receive the magic link email
The magic link says it's expired
I'm getting redirected to the login page repeatedly
The bot appears offline in Discord
The bot is online but not responding
I was charged but my agent isn't working
How do I get a refund?

body:
Login & authentication

I didn't receive the magic link email

- Check your spam, junk, or promotions folder
- Verify you entered the correct email address
- Wait 60 seconds before requesting another link -- there's a cooldown
- If using a corporate email, your IT department may be blocking the sender
- Try a different email provider (Gmail, Outlook) if issues persist

The magic link says it's expired

Magic links expire after a short time for security. Request a new one from the login page. Don't bookmark or save magic link URLs -- they're single-use.

I'm getting redirected to the login page repeatedly

Your session may have expired. Clear your browser cookies for Pantheon and request a new magic link. If this happens frequently, check that your browser allows cookies.

---

Discord connectivity

The bot appears offline in Discord

1. Check your dashboard -- verify your workspace shows "Active" status
2. Check that the Pantheon bot hasn't been kicked from the server. Re-add it from Settings > Discord
3. Try sending a DM to the bot to rule out channel permission issues

The bot is online but not responding

- Make sure you're messaging in a channel the bot has access to. Check the bot's role permissions in Discord server settings.
- Verify the bot has these permissions: Send Messages, Read Message History, Read Messages/View Channels, Embed Links, Attach Files
- If using channel bindings, make sure the channel you're in is bound to an agent or is handled by the default agent
- Check your dashboard for any error indicators

The bot responds slowly

- First response in a new conversation may take a few extra seconds
- Data-heavy tasks (comparisons, report generation) may take longer as your agent processes multiple sources
- Image processing tasks require additional time for analysis
- If consistently slow, check your usage dashboard for any unusual patterns

The first response in a conversation may be slightly slower as your agent loads context. Subsequent responses will be faster.

---

Custom skills

Skill not triggering

- Verify the skill is in Active status (not Draft or Archived)
- Check that the skill is assigned to the agent handling the channel you're messaging in
- Review the skill's trigger description -- make sure your message matches the intended use case
- Test the skill from the Skill Forge test panel to isolate the issue

AI-generated skill needs improvement

- Edit the skill in the Forge editor and refine the instructions
- Add more specific examples in the Examples section
- Attach reference files for additional context
- Regenerate with a more detailed description

---

Setup

Workspace setup seems stuck

Workspace creation typically completes in a few seconds. If it takes longer:

- Refresh your dashboard page
- Check that your subscription is active
- Try logging out and back in

If the issue persists for more than a few minutes, contact support.

Workspace shows an error

Common causes:

- The Pantheon bot may have been removed from your Discord server -- re-add it from Settings > Discord
- Your subscription may have lapsed -- check Settings > Billing

---

Billing

I was charged but my agent isn't working

- Check your workspace status on the dashboard first
- If the workspace shows an error, try the setup troubleshooting steps above
- Your subscription is valid regardless of workspace status -- you won't lose your billing period

How do I get a refund?

Contact support through your dashboard. Refund requests are handled on a case-by-case basis.

Unexpected high usage charges

Check your usage dashboard at Settings > Usage:

- Look for spikes in daily token usage
- Automated cron jobs (scheduled briefings and reports) consume tokens on schedule even if you don't read them
- Multiple agents each consume tokens independently
- Image processing uses significantly more tokens than text-based interactions

To reduce usage, consider disabling unused cron jobs and using text input instead of images when possible. See the Metered Usage guide for more tips.

title: Troubleshooting
body:
Getting help

If you run into issues with Pantheon, this section covers the most common problems and their solutions. For issues not covered here, contact support through your dashboard.

Quick diagnostics

Before diving into specific issues, check these basics:

1. Workspace status — Check your dashboard. Your workspace should show "Active" status.
2. Discord bot — Verify the bot appears online (green dot) in your Discord server's member list.
3. Subscription — Confirm your subscription is active in Settings > Billing.

If all three check out and you're still having issues, read on for specific scenarios.

Common categories

- Common Issues — Login problems, Discord connectivity, skill errors, billing questions, and more
- Workflow Builder Support Runbook — Incident triage and mitigation for workflow publish/run failures

title: Workflow Builder Support Runbook
body:
Workflow Builder Support Runbook

Use this runbook when workflow builder behavior impacts operators or production runs.

Severity model

- P1: publish failures or run failures across multiple customers with active impact.
- P2: single-customer publish/run failures that block operations.
- P3: degraded UX with available workaround.

First-response checklist (first 15 minutes)

1. Confirm incident scope: customer IDs, instance IDs, workflow IDs, run IDs.
2. Check /settings/workflows/launch for KPI spikes and gate failures.
3. Validate rollout target ring and customer assigned ring.
4. Inspect latest run timeline, step errors, and artifact events.
5. Classify incident severity and assign incident commander.

API checks

- GET /api/instances/:id/workflow-runs
- GET /api/instances/:id/workflow-runs/:runId
- POST /api/instances/:id/workflow-runs/:runId/retry-step
- POST /api/instances/:id/workflows/:workflowId/rollback

Immediate mitigations

- Pause ring promotion by setting WORKFLOWBUILDERROLLOUTTARGETRING to prior ring.
- For run-time regressions, rollback affected workflows to prior published version.
- For validation regressions, block publish and route users to manual run procedures.

Escalation path

1. Support on-call
2. Workflow platform engineer
3. Runtime/infrastructure on-call
4. Product owner for customer comms

Incident close-out

1. Document root cause and recovery timeline.
2. Link impacted run IDs and workflows.
3. Update weekly KPI review agenda with follow-up actions.
4. Add regression coverage for the triggering condition.
